winners.title: Watershed Watch — a basin-scoped environmental deadline-and-action radar for volunteer conservation groups
winners.tagline: Never miss the federal or state environmental decision affecting your creek — and turn your members into the radar for everything local.
winners.the_problem: Small volunteer-run watershed alliances, friends-of-the-creek groups, and land trusts are chronically late to the fights that matter most: a draft EIS on an upstream project, a stormwater/wetlands permit comment window, an EPA or state-agency rulemaking, a floodplain rezoning. The signals are scattered across Regulations.gov dockets, the Federal Register, the EPA EIS database, state legislation, and the fragmented local long tail. These groups have passion but no policy staff, so they miss short windows, file weak duplicate comments officials discount, and burn out because they cannot see whether any of it mattered. Existing tools are either lobbyist B2B (Quorum/Plural), Congress-only action (Resistbot/5 Calls), or government-side engagement portals (Granicus) — none deliver discovery + deadline + quality action + honest receipts to a non-expert volunteer scoped to a watershed.
winners.target_user: Primary: volunteer leaders and 1-3 person staff of geographically-scoped conservation groups — watershed alliances, Waterkeeper affiliates, land trusts, friends-of-the-creek/-bay/-forest groups — who own a defined basin and have no policy capacity. Secondary: statewide environmental coalitions and regional water-board NGOs who buy org seats and act as the funded channel partner; and local newsrooms/other nonprofits who license the federal-deadline confidence feed.
winners.the_solution: Watershed Watch is a grant- and coalition-funded deadline-and-action service scoped to a single watershed. The user picks their basin from the USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (a free national HUC primitive — no bespoke boundary drawing needed) and the system continuously catches federal and state environmental decisions whose geography is carried in standardized notice text and metadata: EPA EIS filings (searchable by state, with a fixed 45-day-draft / 30-day-final comment clock), Regulations.gov dockets and Federal Register notices, and state environmental legislation via Open States. Each hit becomes a first-class, alertable deadline object — comment-period close dates rendered as a calendar with lead-time reminders, which exists nowhere today. For each open window the user gets a plain-language brief and a structured-evidence comment composer that produces substantive, non-duplicate testimony officials actually weigh. The monitor->act loop closes with honest procedural receipts: filed-on-time confirmations, comment counts, and draft-to-final text diffs — never causal "you changed this" claims. The local long tail it cannot reliably scrape is handled by the members themselves: a NoticeNail-style flow lets volunteers photograph a posted site notice, geocode the parcel once, and surface it to the group — explicitly labeled "community signal, not comprehensive monitoring." Pol.is consensus is held in reserve only for genuinely contested multi-faction fights.
how_it_works: Onboard: the group picks its watershed from the USGS WBD (HUC-8/12) via The National Map REST service; the system resolves the intersecting states and counties so it knows which jurisdictions to watch.

Ingest Tier 1 (automated): poll Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register API + the EPA EIS database + Open States v3 for environmental dockets/bills; RuleBox-style LLM classification rules extract geographic references and watershed-relevance, scoped to the group's HUC, states, and named water bodies.
Normalize + dedupe into the Open Civic Data model, attach the comment-period close date as a first-class deadline object, and write to a versioned archive (MAPLE pattern) with a Typesense index.

Alert + brief: the follow->event->feed->digest engine pushes a plain-language brief and a deadline-with-lead-time reminder to the group; coverage is labeled per area (Tier 1 reliable / Tier 3 best-effort / Tier 2 community-reported).

Act: the structured-evidence comment composer guides volunteers to a substantive, non-duplicate comment, captures address->district for real-person assurance, and files to Regulations.gov / agency portal, recording a filed-on-time receipt.

See impact honestly: the group's dashboard shows procedural receipts — comments filed, total docket comment count, and a draft-to-final text diff when the agency publishes — with no manufactured causality. Members add local long-tail signals via the site-notice capture flow.

builds_on.name: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD / HUC) + The National Map REST service
builds_on.what: Verified free national watershed-boundary primitive queryable at hydro.nationalmap.gov ArcGIS REST; turns 'draw your basin' into 'pick a HUC and intersect with state/county boundaries,' eliminating the bespoke-boundary problem the critique feared.

builds_on.name: EPA EIS Database + Federal Register API
builds_on.what: Verified searchable by state/territory with a standardized comment-period clock (45-day draft EIS / 30-day final); this is the geo-bearing, deadline-bearing federal stream that makes Tier 1 a real product on day one.

builds_on.name: Regulations.gov v4 + Mirrulations + spicy-regs
builds_on.what: commentEndDate becomes the deadline object; Mirrulations S3 mirror + spicy-regs Parquet/DuckDB/MCP avoid the 1000/hr rate limit and let an LLM query the full corpus via SQL for relevance tuning and backfill.

builds_on.name: Open States / Plural v3
builds_on.what: State environmental legislation + hearings via updated_since polling and people.geo for the represent-me layer, scoped to the watershed's intersecting states.

builds_on.name: mySociety RuleBox + TheyWorkForYou vector alerts + WriteToThem
builds_on.what: RuleBox generates cheap LLM classification rules to scope docket text to a basin at firehose scale; vector-search topic alerts auto-expand related terms; WriteToThem's postcode->reps pattern hides the 'who represents you' complexity for non-experts.

builds_on.name: MAPLE patterns
builds_on.what: Firestore/Cloud Functions + self-hosted Typesense indexer + separate LLM enrichment service + the follow->event->feed->digest notification engine + versioned public testimony archive — the proven spine for the monitor and archive halves.

builds_on.name: NoticeNail-style capture + MapIt clone
builds_on.what: Volunteer photo-of-posted-notice + one-time parcel geocode is the human sensor for the local long tail; a MapIt-style boundary->jurisdiction lookup grounds 'what governs this parcel.'

builds_on.name: Legistar OData + City Scrapers (Tier 3, best-effort)
builds_on.what: Opportunistic committee/keyword matching where a city is on Legistar; explicitly labeled best-effort because, as the critique verified, Legistar matters carry no parcel/geocode.

builds_on.name: Pol.is / Computational Democracy
builds_on.what: Reserved for genuinely contested multi-faction fights with a diverse pool; not the v1 default for a 12-person single-mission group.

winners.gap_filled: Leads with Gap 1 (comment-period deadlines as a first-class alertable object) on the highest-stakes, hardest-to-track, genuinely geo-bearing stream for these users — federal/state environmental dockets — which is net-new whitespace nobody renders as an alertable deadline today. It also hits Gap 3 (cross-source normalization into one watershed feed), Gap 4 (plain-language + guided action), and Gap 5 (the full monitor->act->see-impact loop in one consumer product with a built-in sustainability answer). It touches Gap 2 (land use) honestly via the community-reported sensor layer rather than pretending to scrape it. Not a duplicate: it is geography-and-mission-scoped for amateurs, unlike lobbyist B2B incumbents; it is constituent-side, unlike Granicus/PublicInput; and it integrates the environmental docket + deadline + organize + impact loop that City Tracker AI/Symbium slivers lack.
winners.mvp_scope: Smallest credible v1: pick a basin from the USGS WBD; automated Tier 1 monitoring of EPA EIS filings + Regulations.gov + Federal Register + Open States, scoped by HUC/state, with each open comment window rendered as a deadline object with email/SMS lead-time reminders; a plain-language brief and a structured-evidence comment composer that files to Regulations.gov with a filed-on-time receipt; and a simple dashboard showing comment counts + draft-to-final diffs. Defer NoticeNail capture, Pol.is, and all Legistar/Accela ingestion to fast-follows behind clear coverage labels. First geography/domain: ONE basin with ONE committed coalition partner — strongest candidate is the Chesapeake Bay watershed via a Waterkeeper affiliate or a Chesapeake Bay Foundation chapter (dense federal/state EIS and permit activity, an organized funded coalition, multi-state HUC that exercises the cross-jurisdiction logic), or as a smaller alternative, San Francisco Bay via Baykeeper.
winners.why_now: Housing and infrastructure pressure is pushing development onto floodplains and wetlands, raising the volume and stakes of environmental reviews exactly when 2023 NEPA amendments imposed shorter, harder deadlines that make a missed window unrecoverable. Cheap LLM classification (RuleBox-style rules on commodity infra) finally makes firehose-scale geo-scoping of docket text affordable to a foundation-tier budget, and the federal/state environmental corpus is now fully mirrored and query-ready (Mirrulations + spicy-regs). The geo and deadline primitives that used to be the blocker — USGS WBD boundaries and the EPA EIS state/clock metadata — are free, standardized, and API-accessible today.
winners.business_model: Three legs, channel-led and built into the idea rather than bolted on: (1) Foundation and environmental-justice grants fund the free Tier 1 monitoring for volunteer groups — the public good. (2) Paid org seats sold to staffed land trusts, Waterkeeper affiliates, and statewide coalitions who get multi-chapter admin, custom basins, and member-management — the channel that also drives distribution to their volunteer chapters. (3) A sideways, horizontal "DocketClock" confidence API/feed — the normalized federal/state environmental comment-deadline stream — licensed to other nonprofits and newsrooms; this is the revenue line with the broadest TAM and the least vertical-specific cost. The contradictory original line (selling the activists' dashboard to the opposing water board) is removed.
winners.defensibility: The moat is not the data (all sources are open) but the curated, tuned, watershed-scoped relevance layer plus the trusted distribution channel. Specifically: (a) a continuously human-tuned RuleBox classification corpus that correctly maps docket text to basins and avoids documented traps like BLM "extension/correction" false positives — this accuracy compounds and is expensive for a generalist to replicate; (b) coalition relationships and member-reported local signal that no incumbent has constituent-side; (c) the honest-receipts brand that activists trust. Adjacency risk is real: Granicus owns Legistar + EngagementHQ and could add a "watch this docket" alert, but Granicus is structurally a government-buyer vendor and will not credibly serve groups organizing against its own customers; FiscalNote/Plural is priced and built for lobbyists; Resistbot is Congress-only with no discovery. The thin spot is honest: a generic "DocketClock" alert is the most copyable piece, so defensibility leans on channel + tuned relevance + trust, not on the alert itself.

key_risks.risk: The honest wedge feels underwhelming versus the mission. The most emotionally charged fights (the local floodplain rezoning) are precisely the ones Tier 1 cannot reliably surface, so groups may judge the tool by what it misses, not what it catches.
key_risks.mitigation: Lead messaging on 'never miss a federal/state window again' (a real, demonstrable win) and validate with a design-partner before building that catching upstream EIS/permit windows is itself high-value; treat community-reported local signal as a clearly-labeled bonus, never a promise.

key_risks.risk: Geo-classification accuracy is a permanent tuning burden, not a solved step; the BLM extension/correction trap and watershed-relevance false positives/negatives erode trust fast.
key_risks.mitigation: Ship per-area confidence labels and a human-in-the-loop review queue for the coalition partner's staff; use spicy-regs/DuckDB over the Mirrulations corpus to build and regression-test classification rules against labeled history before going live in a basin.

key_risks.risk: The product is arguably DocketClock + a Waterkeeper skin; the vertical wrapper may not earn distribution or funding the bare registry can't.
key_risks.mitigation: Make the vertical earn its keep through the funded coalition channel and EJ-grant fit that a horizontal registry cannot access; if the vertical fails to add distribution, fall back to monetizing DocketClock horizontally — the architecture supports both.

key_risks.risk: Funding requires landing ONE committed coalition partner before ingestion has value, and environmental-funder appetite for civic infra (vs. direct organizing) is cyclical.
key_risks.mitigation: Secure a signed design partner and a single anchor grant before any basin build; size the MVP so one grant + a handful of org seats reaches break-even for one basin; expand only with a funded partner per basin.

key_risks.risk: The member-reported site-notice layer carries the unsolved paid-QA cost and false-security risk that got NoticeNail deferred.
key_risks.mitigation: Defer it past MVP; when added, keep it strictly 'community signal, not comprehensive monitoring,' push QA to the coalition partner's staff rather than central paid moderation, and never let it drive a coverage guarantee.

key_risks.risk: TAM is a vertical slice of a niche of non-paying volunteers; growth may cap absent the horizontal API revenue.
key_risks.mitigation: Treat org seats + the DocketClock feed as the real revenue engine and the volunteer tier as the mission/funding magnet; expand the feed to adjacent verticals (land trusts broadly, fisheries, air quality) once the environmental basin model is proven.

winners.verdict: A buildable, honest narrowing of a noble-but-overpromised idea: it wins by leading with the one environmental stream where geography and deadlines are actually standardized, and by refusing to fake coverage of the local long tail.

votes.rationale: The post-debate refinements are real improvements: tier-ranking data streams by deliverability, demoting Pol.is to an edge case, and replacing "comprehensiveness" with honest per-area coverage labeling. The federal/state environmental docket layer (Gap 1) is genuinely buildable on Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register + Mirrulations, and treating volunteers as the local sensor layer for the long tail is intellectually honest rather than pretending to scrape Accela. Gap_fit earns a 4 because it touches gaps 1, 3, 4, and 5 with real data, and gestures credibly at gap 2 without overselling it. Feasibility lands at 3: Tier 1 ships, but the geo-classification tuning burden (false positives on docket text vs. a drawn basin polygon) is a continuous trust-critical cost the team has already documented as hard; the human-in-the-loop notice layer still carries unsolved QA costs; and Legistar + opportunistic Socrata/ArcGIS are labeled "best-effort" for good reason. Demand gets a 3 because the target users are real and underserved, but the wedge product — federal/state dockets only, local coverage = what your neighbors report — may feel underwhelming to volunteer leaders whose most urgent fights are local rezonings and permits; adoption before the local layer matures is unproven. Defensibility scores only 2: the core federal/state deadline primitive is essentially DocketClock with a Waterkeeper skin, and as the team's own analysis notes, a horizontal infra player or Granicus extension could replicate the environmental filter without the vertical distribution advantage; the vertical must prove it earns distribution the bare registry cannot, and that is not yet demonstrated. Mission_fit is a solid 4 — the monitor-act-see-impact loop is intact, quality-over-volume comment composition directly addresses the "officials discount form letters" failure mode, and the anti-burnout framing around timeliness and honest procedural receipts is coherent — but it stops short of 5 because the most emotionally charged local fights remain the hardest to surface, leaving a persistent gap between the mission promise and the v1 delivery.

Watershed Watch earns its gap_fit score by genuinely targeting whitespace — federal/state environmental deadline alerting is an unmet, time-critical need for volunteer conservation groups, and the basin-scoped framing is a defensible wedge rather than a me-too registry. Mission_fit is also strong: the monitor-act-impact loop is coherent, the anti-burnout framing is honest, and the funding model (org seats + grants + sideways API licensing) is built-in rather than deferred. However, the idea accumulates risk in three places that cap the score. On feasibility: the refined v1 is genuinely shippable (Regulations.gov + Federal Register + Open States + RuleBox-style LLM geo-classification), but the trust-critical watershed-relevance classifier is explicitly flagged as an ongoing tuning burden — false positives erode exactly the credibility that environmental groups need to stay engaged, and the human-sensor local-capture layer still carries unsolved QA cost even when demoted to Tier 2. On demand: the target user has real pain but is a thin population — staffed Waterkeeper affiliates and land trusts with budget to pay for org seats number in the hundreds nationally, not thousands, and foundation grant cycles for civic-tech infrastructure are notoriously cyclical and competitive; without the sideways DocketClock API doing meaningful revenue, TAM is a niche inside a niche. On defensibility: the vertical wrapper adds limited IP over the horizontal DocketClock primitive, and the environmental vertical's distribution advantage is entirely dependent on landing and retaining one committed coalition partner before the product has proven recall — Granicus or a well-funded EJ tech org could replicate the federal-docket alerting layer faster than the coalition channel matures. The idea is the strongest of a set of focused verticals, not a breakout product; score it as a high-conviction wedge with a modest ceiling rather than a platform.

Watershed Watch earns its gap_fit score by genuinely threading Gaps 1, 3, 4, and 5 — the federal/state environmental deadline primitive is real whitespace, the cross-source normalization into a basin-scoped feed is net-new, and the monitor-act-impact loop with honest procedural receipts is exactly the thesis. The mission_fit is strong: volunteer watershed groups are a textbook non-expert user with real stakes, genuine burnout risk, and no existing tool that combines discovery, guided action, and outcome tracking without requiring policy staff. However, feasibility takes a hit because the idea still bundles three partially-deferred components (the human-in-the-loop site-notice layer, the geo-classification tuning pipeline, and Pol.is consensus for contested fights), each of which carries its own unsolved cost — and the Tier 1-only product (federal/state environmental dockets) is real but narrow enough that watershed groups whose most emotionally salient fights are local rezonings may churn before the full stack lands. Demand scores modestly: the target users are real and passionate, but the addressable population of funded watershed groups and Waterkeeper affiliates with actual budget for civic-tech infrastructure is small, and environmental-funder appetite for tooling (vs. direct organizing) is cyclical and competitive. Defensibility is the weakest point: the core DocketClock deadline primitive is the team's own horizontal infra, and this vertical wrapper adds a watershed-relevance classifier and a Waterkeeper distribution channel — both valuable but neither creating a wide moat; Granicus or a better-funded env-tech nonprofit could replicate the skin. The refined scope discipline (one basin, one committed partner, consume APIs, label coverage honestly) is the right call and saves the idea from the comprehensiveness fantasy that would have killed it, but it also means the product ships feeling narrow while the defensible differentiation accrues slowly through coalition relationships and a tuned geo-classifier that earns trust over repeated docket cycles.

residual_risks: Tier 1 federal/state-only is a real but NARROW product; whether watershed groups will adopt/retain a tool that honestly says 'local coverage = notices you and your neighbors report' is unproven. The most emotionally-charged fights (the local floodplain rezoning) are exactly the ones it can least reliably surface, so the wedge may feel underwhelming versus the mission promise.

The vertical wrapper adds little defensible IP over the team's own DocketClock primitive (which already survived as horizontal infra). Risk that Watershed Watch is just DocketClock + a Waterkeeper-branded skin + deferred components; the environmental vertical must prove it earns distribution/funding the bare registry can't get, not just re-skin it.

Bundles two components the team's own foundry DEFERRED (NoticeNail local capture; Pol.is/Consensus room). The defense rightly demotes both, but the human-in-the-loop site-notice layer still carries the unsolved paid-QA cost and false-security risk that got NoticeNail shelved; it must stay clearly labeled 'community signal, not comprehensive monitoring.'

Funding still requires landing ONE committed coalition partner (regional water board NGO / Waterkeeper affiliate) before ingestion has value; the channel-led org-seats + foundation-grant model is plausible but unvalidated, and environmental-funder appetite for civic-tech infrastructure (vs. direct organizing) is cyclical.

RuleBox-style LLM geo-classification of docket text to a drawn basin will produce false positives/negatives (the BLM 'extension/correction' keyword trap is documented in the team's own mismatch-evidence); watershed-relevance scoping accuracy is a trust-critical, ongoing tuning burden, not a solved step.

TAM remains a vertical slice of a niche; even with org-seat distribution, the number of staffed land trusts / Waterkeeper affiliates with budget is modest, capping growth absent the sideways DocketClock-API revenue line doing the heavy lifting.

winners.title: Closed the Loop — the post-action outcome receipt that ends the not-knowing
winners.tagline: You commented. We watch the docket and text you what actually happened — in plain English, with the source, and your next move.
winners.the_problem: Busy non-experts who submit a comment or testimony hit a wall of silence afterward and conclude their effort was pointless, so they never come back. Agencies do publish response-to-comments and final rules, but no consumer product connects a specific person's submission to that downstream outcome, leaving Resistbot/5 Calls users at "sent" and Regulations.gov users with no follow-up at all. The result is a chronic abandonment problem that quietly kills every other product in this space.
winners.target_user: Primary: the lapsed-but-caring civic actor who commented once on a rule or local issue, never heard back, and went quiet — reachable because a host action product captured their submission. Secondary: the campaign organizer or coalition staffer who must show their group "the work moved something" to keep volunteers mobilized, and the funded payer (advocacy org, agency, or newsroom) who wants a credible accountability signal.
winners.the_solution: Closed the Loop is not an impact-attribution company — the red team correctly kills that. It is a conservative, source-quoting outcome-and-next-step watchdog that lives INSIDE an action flow that owns the submission event. At submission, it captures the user's own comment text plus the Regulations.gov trackingNbr, docketId, and document objectId, so it never has to re-find the comment in the public record (sidestepping the GAO-21-103181 re-find failure where 5-30% of commenters denied submitting and most comments lacked emails). It then watches that specific docket/document; when a decision lands it delivers one plain-language message that (1) reports the outcome with a verbatim govinfo/Federal Register link, (2) at THEME level quotes the agency's own response-to-comments on the user's concern ("the agency responded to this issue, which you and many others raised") — never an individual causal claim — and (3) routes to the next real lever. The monitor half is a reconcile-and-flag deadline/outcome engine over Federal Register + Regulations.gov; the act half is the next-action router (appeal window, related hearing, reintroduction, the decision-maker to lobby). The LLM is a retriever/triager, not a judge: low-confidence or high-stakes matches degrade to "we found the decision, here is the document" rather than a generated claim.
how_it_works: At submission inside the host action product, store the user's comment text, trackingNbr, docketId, and document objectId, plus a follow record keyed to OCD-ID and Regulations.gov identifiers — provenance captured, never reconstructed.

A reconcile-and-flag watcher polls Federal Register API + Regulations.gov v4 (and Mirrulations/spicy-regs for history) on the docket: it chains extension/reopening/correction/withdrawal documents, resolves FR-vs-Regulations.gov date conflicts, and emits a status (pending / final rule / withdrawn / rejected / no-change-yet) with a confidence flag, never silently picking one source.

When a final rule or terminal status posts, a retrieval step pulls the agency's response-to-comments section and finds the THEME matching the user's stored comment text via embeddings over the spicy-regs/Mirrulations corpus — returning the agency's verbatim language, not a generated verdict.

A guardrail layer gates output: high-confidence theme match -> quote the agency + diff; low confidence or high-stakes -> degrade to status-only 'we found the decision, here is the document.' No 'your point mattered' sentence is ever auto-sent.

Deliver one plain-language receipt over the user's messaging channel (SMS/WhatsApp/email) with the verbatim source link, the theme-level quote (or status-only fallback), and exactly one named next action with its real deadline.

Log every receipt to a versioned public archive (MAPLE pattern) so claims are auditable, and feed re-engagement (did they click, did they take the next action) back as the retention metric that proves or kills the thesis.

builds_on.name: Federal Register API + Regulations.gov v4
builds_on.what: Proposed-vs-final diff and the commentEndDate/openForComment/allowLateComments/withdrawn fields are the outcome substrate; docketclock-research/official-semantics.md gives the exact MVP contract (model docket->documents->comments via objectId/commentOnId, display commentEndDate but flag conflicts).

builds_on.name: Mirrulations + spicy-regs (Parquet/DuckDB/MCP)
builds_on.what: Free full mirror of Regulations.gov makes the response-to-comments retrieval and historical diff tractable via SQL/embeddings instead of analyst labor — this is the 'why now' that makes theme matching cheap rather than manual.

builds_on.name: Open States / Plural v3 (updated_since + roll-calls)
builds_on.what: Phase-2 status receipts for state legislation: bill passed/failed/reintroduced via roll-call votes, polled with updated_since; status-only, no causal claim needed.

builds_on.name: OCD-IDs
builds_on.what: Stable join key so a followed item survives across re-dockets, extensions, and source changes over the 1-3 year final-rule timeline — the identifier glue the ACUS report shows agencies routinely break.

builds_on.name: MAPLE follow->event->feed->digest + versioned archive
builds_on.what: Reuse the notification engine pattern and the versioned testimony archive for auditable, replayable receipts; we fix MAPLE's mailto:-only delivery weakness by owning the submission event and the messaging channel.

builds_on.name: RuleBox (mySociety) classification pattern
builds_on.what: LLM-generated classifiers run on cheap infra to triage which docket documents are terminal/extension/correction at firehose scale — used for triage only, never for the per-user attribution call.

winners.gap_filled: Primarily Gap 5 (the see-impact half of the loop that no consumer product owns) and Gap 4 (plain-language legibility). It is not a duplicate: Resistbot/5 Calls stop at 'sent'; Regulations.gov has zero follow-up; Quorum/FiscalNote analytics are B2B lobbyist-facing and never tell an individual constituent what happened to their specific submission; FRTracker (the closest prior art per docketclock-research/prior-art.md) indexes FR + Regulations.gov comments and ships open-comment alerts but has NO post-action, per-user outcome receipt and explicitly no deadline-reconciliation algorithm. The novelty is the glue: provenance captured at submission + conservative theme-level outcome retrieval + next-action routing, delivered as one personal message.
winners.mvp_scope: Start with the Wizard-of-Oz gate BEFORE any build: hand-write outcome receipts for ~200 followers of ONE high-traffic, near-terminal federal docket (an EPA or HUD rule with a known imminent final action, e.g. an item in the EPA-HQ-OW or HUD AFFH lineage that already has response-to-comments posted) and measure re-engagement against a held-out control. First geography/domain: federal rulemaking only, single agency cluster (environment or housing) where final rules are actually landing now — not a slow pipeline. Only if re-engagement moves do you build v1: provenance capture at submission for ONE host action flow, the reconcile-and-flag watcher (FR + Regulations.gov, extension/reopening chaining, conflict flagging), the retrieval+guardrail receipt generator with the status-only fallback as default, and SMS/email delivery. Local/Legistar/land-use outcomes are explicitly OUT of scope until separately validated.
winners.why_now: The proposed-vs-final diff and semantic match of a user's comment to the agency's response-to-comments only became cheap when Mirrulations mirrored the full Regulations.gov corpus to AWS Open Data and spicy-regs wrapped it in Parquet/DuckDB + an MCP server — turning a former analyst task into a tractable retrieval query. Messaging-native delivery (SMS/WhatsApp) and the RuleBox 'LLM generates cheap classifiers' pattern make firehose-scale triage affordable, so the only thing standing between 'sent' and 'here is what happened' is now product discipline, not data availability.
winners.business_model: Funded payer is never the budget-light individual. Three credible buyers, all aligned with the repo's market-signals evidence: (1) advocacy orgs / coalitions running campaigns who pay to retain mobilized volunteers (embedded member-alert workflow, the 'show your group it worked' job — the associations/coalitions segment flagged as high-urgency); (2) newsrooms wanting an accountability signal feed; (3) agencies/B2G via a SocietyWorks-style commercial arm that funds the free consumer surface. Packaging follows the market-signals bands: a Pro embedded feed at $200-$1,000/month for orgs and a team/firm tier at $10k-$30k/year for unlimited members + audit logs + provenance trails. The capability is sold as retention infrastructure for action products, not as a standalone consumer subscription — which the portfolio repeatedly shows fails on funding.
winners.defensibility: The moat is being glued to the submission event and the user relationship, not the data — every source it uses is free/public, so a pure data play has no moat (FRTracker, Apify at $0.35/1k docs, and Regulations.gov itself prove the raw layer is commodity). Because provenance is captured at submission, an incumbent that did NOT own that moment (Quorum, FiscalNote) physically cannot reproduce a clean per-user receipt — they hit the GAO re-find wall. The defensible asset is the proprietary join between (a) a user's stored comment text/trackingNbr and (b) the reconciled outcome, plus the trust earned by a conservative never-overclaim receipt. Honest weakness: the action products that DO own the submission event (Resistbot, 5 Calls, MAPLE) are the real competitive threat and the natural acquirers/hosts — which is exactly why this is positioned as an embedded capability/portfolio sibling rather than a standalone company.

key_risks.risk: The 'void after acting is why people quit' premise is uncited folk-psychology; it is equally plausible people quit because the issue resolved or they never expected one comment to matter, and an honest receipt that usually says 'no change yet' could DEEPEN the void.
key_risks.mitigation: Hard validation gate: the ~$few-hundred Wizard-of-Oz pilot on one docket with a held-out control must show re-engagement lift before any LLM/diff build. If it doesn't move, the idea dies cheap by design.

key_risks.risk: Even theme-level language ('the agency responded to this concern you and many others raised') sits one sentence away from the forbidden individual causal claim, and one over-eager auto-sent sentence about a government action is credibility-destroying and potentially defamatory.
key_risks.mitigation: Default output is status-only; theme quotes are extractive (verbatim agency text + diff only), never generated; a confidence gate degrades high-stakes/low-confidence matches to 'here is the document.' Every receipt is logged to a versioned auditable archive and human-reviewed in the early cohort.

key_risks.risk: The dominant federal outputs are null/rejected/pending over a 1-3 year pipeline — the worst domain for fast retention proof — while the emotionally resonant local land-use wins are out of scope.
key_risks.mitigation: Select the launch docket cluster specifically for near-terminal, already-landing final rules; frame every null/rejected/pending output as a NAMED next action (appeal window, related hearing, reintroduction); add Open States status receipts (faster cadence) as phase 2 to shorten time-to-wow.

key_risks.risk: Per-source outcome data inherits documented traps — multiple e-dockets per rulemaking, extensions/reopenings/corrections as separate documents, FR-vs-Regulations.gov date mismatches, FederalRegister.gov not being legal edition — and routing to a WRONG next-action/deadline is a NEW high-stakes failure the receipt introduces.
key_risks.mitigation: Adopt the reconcile-and-flag contract from official-semantics.md verbatim: chain document lineage, resolve conflicts explicitly, flag rather than silently pick, verify deadlines against govinfo, and treat the next-action router with the same confidence gate as the receipt (degrade to 'check the official notice' when uncertain).

key_risks.risk: As a conceded non-standalone capability, value depends entirely on a host action product that owns the submission event; the portfolio filed it as a deferred module, so its survival is parasitic on a sibling that may never get built.
key_risks.mitigation: Sequence it as the explicit retention layer of whichever action sibling ships first; design the provenance-capture SDK so it is the cheap, obvious add-on at submission time, making the capability the reason an action product wins on retention rather than a floating feature.

key_risks.risk: Funding rests on B2G/newsroom/host-org payers, which is credible but unvalidated and is the same sustainability failure mode the portfolio repeatedly flags (Engage went dormant, vTaiwan unfunded).
key_risks.mitigation: Validate WTP with one paying advocacy-org or newsroom design partner during the Wizard-of-Oz pilot itself (sell the hand-written receipts as a service), so the funding thesis is tested at the same time and cost as the retention thesis.

winners.verdict: It made the cut because the red team rightly kills the impact-attribution COMPANY, but what survives — a conservative, source-quoting, provenance-at-submission outcome watchdog embedded in an action flow — fills the one gap (see-impact) no consumer product owns, is the structural cure for the GAO re-find failure, and dies cheap if the retention premise is wrong.

votes.rationale: Gap 5 (monitor-act-see-impact loop closure) is real and genuinely unowned — no consumer product connects a specific submission to a downstream outcome — so gap_fit earns a 4. The architecture is also admirably disciplined: capturing trackingNbr/docketId at submission time rather than re-finding comments in the public record sidesteps the GAO reconstruction failure, and constraining v1 to federal rulemaking keeps the scope honest. Mission_fit is strong (4) because "end the void" is exactly the retention problem that kills civic engagement for busy non-experts. Feasibility drops to 3 for two compounding reasons: federal rulemaking final rules take 1-3 years and are the worst domain for fast retention proof, and the theme-level LLM diff (proposed-vs-final, "was my concern addressed") is genuinely hard to keep honest at scale — one over-eager auto-generated sentence about a government outcome is a credibility-destroying event that requires permanent UX/framing discipline as an operational cost, not a one-time design decision. Demand is uncertain (3): the "void is why people quit" premise is uncited folk-psychology, the Wizard-of-Oz pilot is correctly identified as a hard gate, and if federal-rulemaking is the only emotionally resonant v1 domain, the slow null-outcome pipeline may not generate enough re-engagement signal to validate the thesis before runway runs out. Defensibility is a weak 3: the structural moat (glued to the submission event and user relationship) is real but parasitic — the capability has no independent existence without a host action product that owns the submission event, and if that sibling never gets built or gets acquired, the loop-closure layer is stranded; meanwhile Granicus or a newsroom data team could ship a simpler status-notification feature into their existing government-buyer relationships faster than a consumer startup can acquire and retain lapsed civic actors.

The idea correctly identifies Gap 5 (the see-impact void) as genuinely unoccupied — no consumer product closes the post-action loop — and the intellectual honesty about what "impact" can and cannot mean (theme-level correlation, not causation; status watchdog, not attribution engine) is a real maturation over naive versions of this idea. The Wizard-of-Oz gate before building is exactly the right instinct. That said, four structural weaknesses drag the score: (1) Demand is the weakest link — the premise that "void after acting is why people quit" is still plausible folk psychology, not evidence, and federal rulemaking (1-3 year final rules, mostly-null outcomes) is the worst possible domain for fast retention validation; the emotionally resonant local land-use wins that would actually re-engage a lapsed neighbor are explicitly deferred. (2) Feasibility is harder than it reads — the Federal Register proposed-vs-final diff looks clean on paper, but catching extensions, reopenings, partial final rules, and FR-vs-Regulations.gov mismatches reliably enough to route a user to the RIGHT next action (not a stale deadline) is operationally brutal; a wrong next-action receipt is a credibility-destroying failure mode the product introduces that didn't exist before. (3) As a conceded non-standalone capability, the product's survival is parasitic on a sibling action product that owns the submission event — it has no independent existence, and the B2G/newsroom funding model is asserted, not validated. (4) The adjacency-creep threat from Granicus and Regulations.gov is real but the structural moat — owning the submission event and storing trackingNbr at action time — is sound in theory but only as strong as the host product's user relationship; if that sibling stalls, the moat evaporates. Mission fit is genuinely high because closing the loop is the single most cited reason civic participation decays, and the framing — "here is the outcome, here is what to do next" — is the right answer to the right question; it just depends on whether the demand hypothesis survives a real pilot.

This idea earns real credit for targeting the most honest and underserved gap in the space — Gap 5, the post-action void — and for doing so with genuine intellectual discipline: no individual causation claims, provenance captured at submission rather than reconstructed, a mandatory Wizard-of-Oz gate before any LLM spend, and an explicit federal-only v1 scope. The gap_fit score is the strongest here because no existing consumer product (Resistbot, Regulations.gov, MAPLE, Quorum) closes this loop for a non-expert individual, and the "next-action routing on null/rejected outcomes" reframe is genuinely clever. Mission fit is solid: the honest receipt model and anti-astroturf design philosophy are exactly what civic tech needs more of, and the quality-over-volume posture is the right instinct. However, three axes pull the score down materially. Feasibility is a 3 because the technical dependencies are real (Regulations.gov proposed-vs-final diffs, spicy-regs LLM retrieval, Open States polling) but the operational burden of permanent framing discipline — ensuring no auto-generated sentence ever crosses into individual causation — is a continuous human-in-the-loop cost that the idea acknowledges but underestimates; one bad batch message about a government action is a credibility event. Demand is also a 3: the "void after acting" retention thesis remains the idea's own admitted uncited folk-psychology, and the federal rulemaking domain — 1-to-3-year final rule timelines, mostly-null outcomes — is the worst possible proving ground for fast retention loops; the emotionally legible local outcomes (zoning, permits) that would generate real re-engagement signals are explicitly out of scope for v1, leaving the pilot test to validate the thesis in the slowest, least resonant domain. Defensibility is a 3 because the product's structural moat (owning the submission event) is parasitic on a host action product that does not yet exist in this portfolio; as a standalone capability it is a feature Resistbot or MAPLE could ship, not a company, and the B2G/newsroom funding model, while credible in theory, has not been validated against an actual paying buyer in this context — which is exactly the sustainability failure mode the constraints section flags across every prior project in this space.

residual_risks: The 'void after acting is why people quit' premise is still uncited folk-psychology; the cheap Wizard-of-Oz pilot (hand-written receipts to ~200 followers on one docket) must be a hard gate before any LLM/diff build, and it could disconfirm the whole retention thesis.

Even the honest theme-level receipt ('the agency responded to this concern you and many others raised') sits on a thin line from the forbidden causal claim; framing/UX discipline is a permanent operational burden, and one over-eager auto-sent sentence about a government action is still credibility-destroying.

As a conceded non-standalone capability, value depends entirely on a host action product that owns the submission event — but the repo's own portfolio already filed it as a deferred module, so it has no independent existence and its survival is parasitic on a sibling that may or may not get built.

The next-action routing (appeal deadline, related hearing, re-introduction) is the real retained value, but that data is itself subject to all the documented traps (extensions/reopenings as separate docs, FR-vs-Regulations.gov mismatches, lagging local minutes) — and routing to a WRONG next-action/deadline is a new high-stakes failure mode the receipt introduces.

Federal-rulemaking-only v1 plus Open States status second is honest but narrows the addressable wow-moments to the slow, mostly-null federal pipeline (1-3yr final rules), which is the worst domain for fast retention proof; the emotionally resonant local land-use outcomes are explicitly out of scope.
Funding still rests on B2G/newsroom/host-org payers rather than the budget-light individual — credible but unvalidated, and the same sustainability failure mode the portfolio repeatedly flags.

winners.title: Permit Pulse
winners.tagline: A two-tier neighborhood land-use digest that joins "what's being built near you" to "where you can still object, and by when."
winners.the_problem: The development that reshapes a neighborhood is rarely a dramatic rezoning — it's the steady drip of demolitions, ADUs, by-right additions, curb cuts, grading permits, and alcohol/cannabis licenses that each fly under the radar but collectively change a block. Residents have no aggregated, legible view, and for the minority of items where input still has a legal window (design review, variances, conditional-use permits, license objections), that window is short, buried in a hearing agenda, and never surfaced as a deadline. The raw data exists in Socrata/Accela/ArcGIS as cryptic permit codes residents can't parse, and the hearing schedule lives separately in Legistar/Granicus agendas.
winners.target_user: Primary: neighborhood-association leaders and engaged residents who want a low-effort "what's changing around here" feed plus a clear signal on the rare items where their input still has a legal window. Secondary: local small-business owners (watching nearby competitor licenses, grading, and street work) and, on the revenue side, the incumbents who want the normalized corpus (real estate, insurance, BuildZoom-type lead tools) and city planning departments wanting better resident noticing.
winners.the_solution: Permit Pulse is the join product between the local permit/license firehose and the Planning/Zoning/Design-Review agenda calendar. It ingests each city's permit feed through interchangeable adapters (Socrata, Accela, ArcGIS — BLDS is treated as one possible source, never the thesis), and normalizes cryptic rows into resident-meaningful categories using RuleBox-style LLM-generated classification rules that then run cheaply at firehose scale. Separately it pulls structured meeting events and agenda items from the Legistar/Granicus OData API (the events endpoint gives real future hearing dates; an LLM parses the free-text/PDF body to extract the case, address, and project type), then joins the two by address and parcel. The product surfaces two honestly-labeled tiers: an AWARENESS feed of by-right activity ranked by impact x proximity (sold purely as early warning and neighbor-organizing, with no false promise of a lever) and a LEVER feed of discretionary items carrying a real hearing date, an inferred comment/appeal window, and a routed action path that can escalate into a "Block Whip" neighbor campaign. Each weekly digest leads with the honest headline — "Nothing needs you this week" or "2 items have an open window" — making candor the trust mechanism, backed by a published, transparent coverage map so misses read as known boundaries rather than failures.
how_it_works: Resident enters an address; MapIt-style geocoding resolves it to parcel, council district, and the relevant planning/zoning bodies, and sets a proximity radius (default: same block + adjacent parcels, expandable).

Permit adapters poll the city's Socrata/Accela/ArcGIS feed; RuleBox-generated rules classify each raw row into a resident category (teardown, ADU, big addition, curb cut, license) and an impact score; new nearby items land in the AWARENESS feed.

A parallel Legistar/Granicus job pulls upcoming Planning Commission / Zoning Board / Design Review events with real EventDate values, then an LLM extracts case number, address, project type, and any stated comment deadline from each agenda item body and its PDF attachments.

A matching layer fuzzy-joins permit rows to agenda cases by normalized address + parcel; matched discretionary items become LEVER-feed objects with a hearing date and a jurisdiction-specific inferred comment/appeal-window deadline.

The weekly digest leads with an honest count ('Nothing needs you this week' vs 'N items have an open window'); lever items show a Cofacts-style cached plain-language explainer of the jargon and a one-tap routed action (write the body that takes comment, sign up to speak, file an objection).

If a resident acts, they can launch a Block Whip — invite neighbors on the same block to act before the deadline — and afterward the item is marked with its disposition (approved/continued/denied) so users see what happened, the honest version of 'see the impact.'

builds_on.name: Legistar / Granicus OData API
builds_on.what: The load-bearing source for the LEVER tier: the events endpoint exposes real future hearing dates (EventDate) and eventitems link matters to a specific meeting, so an upcoming Planning/Zoning/Design-Review hearing date is structurally obtainable for the ~70% of large cities/counties Legistar covers — auth-free. Comment deadlines are NOT a native field and must be LLM-extracted from agenda bodies/PDFs or inferred from statute.

builds_on.name: RuleBox (mySociety)
builds_on.what: LLM generates the classification rules once, then cheap infra runs them on the permit firehose — the only economical way to translate millions of cryptic permit/license rows into resident-meaningful, impact-scored categories per city. This is the genuine, verified why-now, replacing the dead BLDS thesis.

builds_on.name: Permit adapters (Socrata / Accela / ArcGIS, BLDS optional)
builds_on.what: Treated as interchangeable input adapters normalized to OCD, NOT as a unifying standard. Confirmed: BLDS is officially 'not being actively maintained,' so the design assumes bespoke per-city schemas and prices that maintenance in rather than pretending a cross-city schema exists.

builds_on.name: Open Civic Data (OCD) model + OCD-IDs
builds_on.what: Canonical internal schema for jurisdictions, bodies, events, and people so permit rows and agenda cases share IDs and the address->who-governs-me resolution is consistent across adapters.

builds_on.name: TheyWorkForYou alert + digest pattern
builds_on.what: Concept-subscription (subscribe to 'teardowns within 2 blocks' or 'cannabis licenses') plus the weekly-digest delivery cadence and the honest 'nothing this week' lead-in.

builds_on.name: Cofacts cached-answer pattern
builds_on.what: Community-verified, cached plain-language explainers of permit codes and process steps that get cheaper per query over time, powering the legibility layer without per-query LLM cost.

builds_on.name: spicy-regs DuckDB + MCP pattern
builds_on.what: Model for storing the normalized permit+agenda+deadline corpus as queryable Parquet/DuckDB with an MCP layer, which doubles as the licensable B2B API product.

builds_on.name: MapIt (mySociety)
builds_on.what: The address -> parcel/district/governing-body primitive that drives both onboarding ('what governs me') and the proximity ranking.

winners.gap_filled: Primarily fills Gap 3 (cross-source normalization of raw permit vocabulary for non-experts) and Gap 4 (plain-language legibility + guided action), and on matched discretionary items realizes Gap 1 (a first-class, alertable land-use action window) by deriving it from Legistar hearing dates rather than pretending it lives in permit feeds. It is novel because no existing player joins the permit firehose to the hearing calendar and labels the by-right/discretionary split honestly: buildingeye/BuildZoom are search and lead tools with no impact scoring or action window, coUrbanize/City Tracker AI are per-project page slivers, and Nextdoor has distribution but no structured land-use data layer. The defensible asset is exactly the unglamorous join + normalization that adjacency-creep competitors have no incentive to build.
winners.mvp_scope: One metro, end to end, with rigorous honesty over breadth. Suggested first geography: a city that has BOTH a clean Socrata permit feed AND Legistar planning agendas — Los Angeles is the strongest single candidate (rich Socrata permit + license data, Granicus/Legistar agendas, dense neighborhood-council infrastructure that gives a built-in organized-resident wedge), with Chicago as the alternative (mature Socrata permits, City Council on Legistar). MVP: (1) one permit adapter + RuleBox classification into ~8 resident categories with impact x proximity ranking; (2) Legistar events pull for the Planning Commission / Area Planning Commissions / one design-review body, with LLM agenda-item extraction; (3) address-based onboarding, a weekly email/SMS digest leading with the honest 'nothing needs you' / 'N open windows' headline; (4) the LEVER tier for ONLY the cleanest discretionary item type to start (e.g., conditional-use permits or discretionary review) with a hand-verified deadline so the first windows are never wrong; (5) a public coverage map stating exactly what is and isn't tracked. Defer Block Whip, multi-city, and corpus licensing to post-validation.
winners.why_now: RuleBox-style LLM rule-generation now makes it economical to do the two previously-uneconomical jobs at once: translate millions of cryptic permit/license rows into impact-ranked resident categories, AND parse free-text/PDF planning agendas into structured cases with hearing dates. That is the real why-now — explicitly NOT BLDS, which is confirmed officially abandoned ('not being actively maintained') and never reached cross-city scale. Cheap, reliable LLM extraction is what turns a heroic per-city scraping operation into a tractable adapter-plus-classifier pipeline.
winners.business_model: Free consumer digest is top-of-funnel and the trust/distribution engine. Revenue comes from three layers designed in from the start: (1) org/HOA/neighborhood-association campaign tiers (Block Whip campaign tooling, multi-watchlist, faster alerts) — modest ARPU but the warmest buyers; (2) licensing the normalized permit + agenda + deadline corpus/API (DuckDB/MCP-backed) to real-estate, insurance, and lead-gen incumbents who want clean structured land-use signal they won't build themselves; (3) a SocietyWorks-style B2G 'better resident noticing' product sold to planning departments, which both funds the free side and improves source-data access. Honest caveat: each path competes with or depends on an incumbent it cites, and buildingeye/AgencyCounter already sit in the B2G noticing slot — so monetization is sequenced after consumer demand is proven, not assumed.
winners.defensibility: The moat is the unglamorous, compounding join + normalization: per-city permit-adapter mappings, RuleBox classification rule sets, agenda-extraction prompts, address/parcel matching, and jurisdiction-specific comment-window inference. Granicus already owns the agenda data but sells to governments and has no incentive to build a consumer cross-jurisdiction resident layer; FiscalNote/Plural are Congress/statehouse B2B and don't touch municipal permits; Nextdoor owns distribution but has no structured land-use data and no deadline engine. The defensibility is real but slow-building and city-by-city — it is depth and accuracy of normalization, not a one-time data grab, and it must be paired with a distribution answer because the cold-start problem is genuine.

key_risks.risk: The action-window deadline is not a clean field anywhere. Legistar gives a real hearing date via events, but comment deadlines and appeal windows live in free-text agenda bodies, PDF attachments, and per-jurisdiction statute — extraction is fragile and the permit-to-agenda address/parcel match is unsolved at scale. A wrong deadline is worse than no deadline.
key_risks.mitigation: For the MVP lever tier, hand-verify deadlines for ONE clean discretionary item type before showing them; surface the hearing date (reliable) prominently and the comment deadline as derived/'confirm with city' until inference is proven; treat the matcher as best-effort with a human-review queue and never auto-promote an unmatched item to the lever feed.

key_risks.risk: The lever tier is structurally small and most listed permit types are by-right with no objection mechanism, so 'see the impact' is the weakest leg of the loop and official discounting blunts even genuine windows.
key_risks.mitigation: Sell the AWARENESS feed honestly as early-warning and neighbor-organizing with explicitly no lever promise; reserve 'act' for genuine discretionary windows; define impact as disposition tracking (what happened to the case) plus organizing outcomes, not as 'we changed the vote.' Lead every digest with the honest count to set expectations.

key_risks.risk: Demand and willingness-to-pay for a hyperlocal consumer feed are unproven, and the target persona is small, low-frequency, and low-ARPU; named B2B/B2G buyers each compete with or depend on the incumbents cited, with buildingeye/AgencyCounter already in the B2G slot.
key_risks.mitigation: Validate consumer pull in one metro via the organized wedge (LA neighborhood councils / a city's NA federation) before building monetization; treat corpus licensing and B2G as post-validation hypotheses, not the plan; instrument open/click/act rates to prove engagement before pitching any buyer.

key_risks.risk: Coverage is a per-city patchwork (adapter + agenda parser + matcher), the documented sustainability failure mode; trust erodes the instant a digest misses a real teardown.
key_risks.mitigation: Stay deep in 1-3 metros, publish a rigorous coverage map so every gap is a stated boundary, alert users to known blind spots in-product, and only expand a city when both feeds clear an accuracy bar — depth-first growth, not land-grab.

key_risks.risk: Nextdoor owns the engaged-resident distribution channel and the product has a real cold-start problem the incumbents do not.
key_risks.mitigation: Go through organized intermediaries (neighborhood-association federations, council offices) rather than cold consumer acquisition; make the honest weekly digest forwardable and the coverage/transparency angle a differentiator Nextdoor's noisy feed can't match; consider a Nextdoor-post / newsletter syndication channel rather than competing head-on for the home feed.

winners.verdict: It made the cut despite a 'kill' lean because the refined framing survives the kill-shots by conceding them — dropping the dead BLDS thesis, sourcing the genuine action window from verifiable Legistar hearing dates, and selling awareness honestly rather than overpromising a lever — leaving a narrow but real, defensible whitespace if and only if it stays depth-first in one metro and treats deadline accuracy as sacred.

votes.rationale: Gap fit is genuinely strong: the join between a permit firehose and a Legistar planning agenda — with an honest, alertable action-window deadline — fills Gap 1 and Gap 3 in a way no existing player does, and the two-tier AWARENESS/LEVER framing is intellectually honest about what residents can actually influence. Mission fit is good; the monitor->act cadence is real, though "see the impact" remains a promissory note. The scores drop hard on feasibility: the critical asset — extracting case/address/hearing-date/comment-deadline from Legistar agenda bodies and PDF attachments, then fuzzy-matching those to permit rows by address+parcel — is presented as a JOIN but is actually an unsolved NLP+entity-resolution problem at city scale, far messier than the pitch implies. RuleBox-style LLM rule generation helps classification but does not solve the address matching or the statutory-window inference that varies by jurisdiction. Each new metro is a fresh integration project, and the "1-3 metro" discipline, while wise, means growth is gated on per-city engineering. Demand is plausible but unproven for a consumer product; neighborhood association leaders are real but thin-market, and willingness to pay for a digest is uncertain. The B2G and corpus-licensing revenue paths are more credible than consumer subscriptions but each faces an incumbent with a head start (AgencyCounter/buildingeye for B2G noticing; BuildZoom/ATTOM for data licensing). Defensibility is moderate: the normalization moat is real but replicable by any well-funded player once the value is proven, and Nextdoor's distribution advantage is a genuine cold-start ceiling the product has no answer for. On net, a high-conviction gap thesis dragged down by an underestimated engineering burden per city and an unproven consumer demand curve.

Permit Pulse fills a real and underserved whitespace — the join between raw permit data and discretionary-review action windows is genuinely absent from the market, and the honest two-tier framing (awareness vs. lever) shows intellectual integrity that distinguishes it from vaporware civic tools. Gap fit and mission fit are the genuine strengths. However, feasibility is the Achilles heel: the pitch's core "JOIN" metaphor obscures what is actually a multi-stage LLM parsing problem with no clean structured output — agenda items live in PDFs and free text, permit codes vary per-city, and the address+parcel fuzzy-match between permit rows and planning cases is an unsolved-at-scale problem that will require per-jurisdiction engineering indefinitely. That is a 2, not a 3. Demand is real but narrow and unproven at paying scale: neighborhood association leaders are a small, cost-sensitive audience, the awareness feed has no lever and therefore limited urgency, and the lever feed covers a structurally small slice of discretionary items with already-low objection efficacy. Willingness to pay for a consumer digest is historically weak in civic tech; the B2B/B2G monetization paths (corpus licensing, B2G noticing tool) are plausible but each competes with or requires cooperation from incumbents (AgencyCounter, Granicus, BuildZoom) who have no incentive to buy a competitor's data layer. Defensibility gets a 3: the normalization moat is real in theory, but Nextdoor controls resident distribution, Granicus could extend noticing features with existing government relationships, and the per-city adapter treadmill is a resource drain that advantages well-capitalized incumbents over time. The transparent coverage map and SocietyWorks-style funding design are exactly the right lessons learned, but they mitigate rather than eliminate the sustainability failure mode that has killed prior civic digests.

Permit Pulse fills a genuine and underserved gap — the join between raw permit data and discretionary hearing deadlines is exactly the kind of cross-source normalization (Gap 3) and plain-language legibility layer (Gap 4) that no incumbent has built for consumers. The two-tier framing (AWARENESS vs. LEVER) is intellectually honest and the "Nothing needs you this week" / "N items have an open window" digest pattern is the right trust mechanic. However, the feasibility score is the anchor dragging the total down: the core value proposition — extracting a structured hearing date, case number, and comment deadline from Legistar agenda items — lives primarily in PDF attachments and free-text fields, not in clean OData fields, and the fuzzy address+parcel join between a permit row and an agenda case is a genuinely hard matching problem that the pitch understates. LLM-generated RuleBox-style classification rules help on permit vocabulary but they do not solve the extraction-from-PDF or the cross-table entity resolution problem, both of which require per-city validation work that compounds with each new metro. Demand is real but segmented: neighborhood association leaders and HOA members are a real paying constituency, but the total addressable consumer base willing to pay for a hyperlocal digest is narrow, and the B2G and corpus-licensing revenue paths each require a separate sales motion against incumbents (Granicus, BuildZoom) with established relationships. Defensibility is modest: the normalization moat is real if executed, but Nextdoor's distribution advantage and Granicus's direct government-buyer relationships mean the product must win the data layer before it can build distribution — a sequencing risk. Mission fit is strong because the product genuinely serves the non-expert monitor-to-act loop, names its own limits honestly, and avoids the astroturf failure mode by explicitly distinguishing actionable windows from awareness-only items; the "see the impact" leg remains weak but the pitch acknowledges it rather than papering over it.

residual_risks: The action-window deadline is rarely a clean structured field even in Legistar: agenda items frequently live in free-text bodies and PDF attachments, so extracting case/address/hearing-date/comment-deadline requires LLM parsing PLUS statutory-window inference per jurisdiction. This is more fragile than the defense's tidy 'JOIN' framing implies, and the address+parcel fuzzy-match between a permit row and an agenda case is itself an unsolved-at-scale matching problem.

The lever tier is structurally small. By-right items (the explicit 'steady drip' in the pitch) have no objection mechanism, and the critique is correct that official discounting of non-expert objections blunts even the genuine windows. 'See the impact' remains the weakest leg of the monitor->act->see-impact loop; the product must sell awareness/early-warning honestly rather than overpromise outcomes.

Demand and willingness-to-pay for a hyperlocal consumer feed are unproven; the named B2B/B2G monetization paths (corpus licensing to BuildZoom/real-estate/insurance, SocietyWorks-style B2G) are plausible but each competes with or depends on the very incumbents/buyers it cites, and buildingeye/AgencyCounter already occupies the B2G 'notice residents better' slot.

Coverage is still a per-city patchwork (permit adapter + agenda parser + matching), so the sustainability failure mode is mitigated by 1-3 metro scoping and a transparent coverage map but not eliminated; trust erodes on any missed teardown unless the boundary is rigorously communicated.

Nextdoor owns the engaged-resident distribution channel; the structured-data-layer differentiation is defensible in theory but the product still has a cold-start distribution problem the incumbents do not.

winners.title: Consensus Comment
winners.tagline: A public, auditable deliberation room on one federal comment period that turns scattered neighbors into N individually-filed comments plus a reproducible "here's where 80% of us agree" artifact officials can't dismiss as astroturf.
winners.the_problem: When a rule drops, busy residents either do nothing or paste an identical form letter into Regulations.gov — and agencies are explicitly trained to discount identical-text floods as astroturf, so the effort evaporates. The genuinely persuasive output ("here is what 80% of us across the political spectrum actually agree on, in our own words, with our own evidence") never gets produced because nobody runs the deliberation and no tool both clusters opinion AND helps each person file their own attested comment. The informal organizer who can rally 50-300 neighbors can rally bodies but can't synthesize them at docket scale, sees no signal that the work landed, and burns out.
winners.target_user: Primary: the volunteer neighborhood/issue organizer or small-association staffer who can get 50-300 people mildly engaged on a specific federal rule but has no tooling to make that engagement count at docket scale. Secondary: the funded nonprofit / trade association / coalition that already wants evidence-backed comment synthesis and is willing to host and pay for a public-interest room (this is the explicit DEFER-revisit trigger — the anchor must want dissent/provenance, not raw volume).
winners.the_solution: Consensus Comment is a public, auditable deliberation room attached to exactly ONE federal comment period, hosted by a trusted nonprofit/association anchor. It does three things and refuses to do a fourth. (1) It seeds plain-language statements about the docket using an LLM grounded in the agency's own legal text (disclosed as AI-seeded), so a non-expert can react with agree/disagree/pass. (2) It runs open Pol.is-style clustering to surface, with a hard math gate, where participants actually agree across opinion clusters — and it preserves every minority/dissent report rather than burying it. (3) It then helps each participant write and file their OWN individually-attested comment to Regulations.gov by hand, capturing their own receipt — N people, N names, N comments, never one batch packet and never one-click automation. The whole computation — raw votes, cluster math, AI seed prompts, every dissent — is published as a reproducible public method artifact a journalist or clerk can re-run. It consumes its deadline object from DocketClock and never re-derives it. The refusal: no batch packet, no one-click submission, no agency-sold dashboard, no cross-jurisdiction sprawl.
how_it_works: A funded anchor org creates a room and pastes a Regulations.gov docket/document ID; the room binds to that item's DocketClock deadline object (commentEndDate, openForComment, allowLateComments, extension/reopening flags) and renders the operative deadline with source links and an explicit 'file early, not at the wire' nudge.

An LLM grounded in the agency's actual notice/legal text generates 15-25 plain-language seed statements; every seed is labeled AI-generated and the generation prompt is logged to the public artifact. The anchor's facilitator can prune/edit before opening.

Participants vote agree/disagree/pass on statements and can submit their own statements for others to vote on (standard Pol.is loop). Address-to-district geocoding runs as honest aggregate annotation only ('N self-attested District-X residents'), never as a gate or a 'verified' claim.

Open clustering runs continuously. A math gate checks participant count AND cross-cluster diversity: above threshold the room surfaces 'cross-factional consensus' statements plus all minority reports; below threshold it says 'insufficient cross-cluster signal' and degrades to a structured comment-quality workshop.

Each participant uses the consensus frame and dissent reports to draft their OWN comment in their own words (the tool assists structure, never authors the substance), then files it by hand on Regulations.gov and pastes back the tracking/receipt number, which the room logs as aggregate 'N attested filings' with timestamps before the deadline.

On close, the room publishes the immutable public method artifact: raw vote matrix, cluster computation, AI seed prompts, full dissent set, and the disclosed (unverified, honestly-labeled) participant geography — re-runnable by anyone, the credibility carrier the astroturf attack cannot touch.

builds_on.name: DocketClock (sibling foundry SURVIVE product)
builds_on.what: Consumes the resolved federal deadline object — operative date, openForComment/allowLateComments, and first-class extension/reopening/correction/timezone flags — rather than re-deriving it. This is the documented-mismatch hazard (EPA NPDES reopening, the two-actions notice, BLM false-positive, HUD 2025 submission outage) that Consensus Comment must NOT own; per the synthesis, deadline reliability is a separate auditable primitive.

builds_on.name: Pol.is / Computational Democracy Project
builds_on.what: Open opinion-clustering math that surfaces cross-factional consensus (proven in vTaiwan). Adopted as the deliberation engine, but bounded to one short comment window with a hard participant/diversity gate to avoid vTaiwan's months-of-facilitation assumption.

builds_on.name: Mirrulations + spicy-regs (Parquet/DuckDB + MCP)
builds_on.what: Makes the full Regulations.gov corpus and the specific docket cheaply queryable so the LLM can ground statement seeds in the agency's actual notice text and prior comments — the 'why now' that makes credible AI seeding possible without authoring comments.

builds_on.name: Regulations.gov v4 API / Federal Register API
builds_on.what: Used READ-ONLY for docket text, document metadata, and to confirm a comment is filed — explicitly NOT for the rate-limited (50/min, 500/hr) automated submission pipe that the red-team flagged as brittle and liability-laden. Filing stays manual and human.

builds_on.name: MAPLE patterns (versioned public testimony archive; funder-separated)
builds_on.what: Reused for the versioned, public, immutable method/dissent archive and the follow->digest notification engine. We deliberately do NOT inherit MAPLE's mailto:-only delivery or its near-zero verification, downgrading verification to honest annotation.

builds_on.name: MapIt-style address->district geocoding
builds_on.what: Used ONLY to produce aggregate, honestly-labeled district annotation ('N self-attested residents'), never a signer-verification gate — directly heeding GAO-21-103181's finding that even the official record can't reliably attribute comments.

builds_on.name: Open Civic Data IDs
builds_on.what: Each room is keyed to a canonical OCD/docket item so artifacts are addressable, deduplicable, and joinable to DocketClock and future products.

winners.gap_filled: Fills Gap 4 (plain-language legibility + guided action) and Gap 5 (the monitor->act->see-impact loop in one consumer product), and rides Gap 1 by consuming the deadline-as-trigger object instead of rebuilding it. It is novel because no existing tool joins opinion clustering to official comment filing: Pol.is/vTaiwan have no comment-period ingestion or filing pipe; Resistbot/5 Calls do Congress-only volume with no clustering and no discovery; Granicus EngagementHQ is a per-project agency-bought silo with no public reproducible artifact. The deliberate re-scope (no batch packet, no one-click filing, no agency dashboard, no Legistar sprawl) is what distinguishes this from the prior 'Consensus Comment Room' that was DEFER'd — it removes exactly the attack surface (one-entity-files-for-many, automated submission, B2G-to-the-lobbied-agency) the gauntlet warned against, and answers the explicit revisit trigger: a funded nonprofit/association that wants evidence-backed synthesis and accepts dissent/provenance constraints.
winners.mvp_scope: Smallest credible version: ONE federal docket, ONE funded anchor org, no automation. A web room that (a) binds to a DocketClock deadline object, (b) LLM-seeds plain-language statements from the notice text with logged prompts, (c) runs open Pol.is clustering with the 'insufficient cross-cluster signal' gate live from day one, (d) walks each participant through writing-then-manually-filing their own comment and pasting back a receipt, and (e) publishes the immutable public method+dissent artifact on close. No batch packet, no Regulations.gov submission API, no geocoding gate, no agency dashboard, no state/local. First domain/geography: a single EPA or HUD rule with a clear affected-resident constituency (e.g., an EPA environmental-justice or HUD housing rule) where one already-funded national advocacy org or trade association will be the launch anchor and recruit the participants — because without that anchor this is a contingent module, not a fundable bet.
winners.why_now: Mirrulations + spicy-regs now make the full corpus cheaply queryable by an LLM, so credible plain-language statement-seeding grounded in the agency's own text is finally cheap. Pol.is clustering is open and battle-tested. And the macro shift is decisive: agencies' growing, openly-stated fatigue with AI-generated and identical form-letter floods means a credible, transparent, individually-authored quality signal is more valuable in 2026 than raw volume has ever been — the form-letter discount is now the default, not the exception.
winners.business_model: Per-campaign / per-room fees and foundation-funded public-interest rooms, billed to the trusted anchor (nonprofit, trade association, coalition), with the funder structurally separated from moderation and from the consensus math — this is the exact arrangement the adjudicator's revisit trigger required. Honest caveat: this is the weakest leg. Buyers are budget-light and deadline-lumpy, and 'fewer, better' comments reduce the visible volume some funders instinctively value, so pricing must be sold on credibility-and-defensibility-against-astroturf-discounting, not on comment count. No agency-sold dashboard (kills neutrality and invites Granicus adjacency). Most realistic path: launch bundled with/subsidized by DocketClock's professional revenue rather than as a standalone P&L.
winners.defensibility: The moat is reputational and structural, not technical: a track record of public, re-runnable method artifacts that journalists and clerks have actually audited, hosted by trusted neutral anchors. Granicus/FiscalNote/Resistbot can each absorb a feature, but the incumbents' positions structurally conflict with the thing that makes this credible — Granicus sells the comment-intake silo TO the agency being lobbied (capture), FiscalNote/Quorum are lobbyist-facing volume tools (the astroturf brand), and Resistbot does one-click volume (the discounted artifact). Adjacency creep is real but it would force the incumbent to adopt the one posture — public reproducible dissent-preserving artifacts with no batch filing and no agency buyer — that undercuts their existing revenue. Candidly, the moat is asserted until a real clerk/journalist audit demonstrates it; the first such audit is the single most valuable proof point to chase.

key_risks.risk: Legitimacy reframe: even individually-filed, AI-seeded comments can be cast by an opponent as 'coordinated AI advocacy' — the same astroturf discount this product claims to escape, and the reason the prior version was DEFER'd.
key_risks.mitigation: Three structural defenses, not slogans: (1) the human authors every comment's substance — AI only seeds and clusters, logged in the public prompt record; (2) N separate attested filings, never one batch packet; (3) the full re-runnable method+dissent artifact. Pursue an actual clerk/journalist audit of the first room as the proof, since credibility is currently asserted, not demonstrated.

key_risks.risk: The anchor trigger may not be met: there is no evidence in the materials that a funded nonprofit/association wanting dissent-preserving synthesis actually exists, so this remains a contingent module rather than a standalone bet.
key_risks.mitigation: Treat anchor-signing as the literal go/no-go gate before any build. Do not productize standalone; bundle under DocketClock and pursue one named launch anchor (e.g., a national environmental-justice or housing advocacy org) before writing room code.

key_risks.risk: Statistical theater: one 10-60 day window recruited through a single anchor often yields too few or too homogeneous participants for clustering to mean anything, so the headline 'cross-factional consensus' frequently will NOT be produced.
key_risks.mitigation: Ship the hard 'insufficient cross-cluster signal' math gate on day one and make graceful degradation to a structured comment-quality workshop the honest default. Choose a launch rule with a large, genuinely cross-spectrum affected constituency to maximize the chance the gate is cleared.
