key_risks.risk: DocketClock dependency: the only safe way past the deadline-misread hazard (extensions, reopenings, multi-deadline dockets, timezone, the HUD 2025 submission outage) is to consume a deadline object this product doesn't own. If DocketClock slips, this loses its safest foundation.
key_risks.mitigation: Define a strict, versioned deadline-object contract with DocketClock including confidence/flag fields; degrade to read-only display with a loud 'verify the official notice/PDF' disclaimer if confidence is low; never auto-act on a deadline. Always nudge file-early.

key_risks.risk: Self-defeating economics: 'fewer, better' comments reduce the visible volume some funders value, and the buyers are budget-light and deadline-lumpy — funding is weakest exactly where civic value is highest.
key_risks.mitigation: Sell on credibility/defensibility against the form-letter discount, not on count; price per-room/foundation-funded with funder-moderation separation; subsidize via DocketClock professional revenue rather than standing alone on a thin P&L.

key_risks.risk: Verification can only ever be honest annotation (GAO-21-103181: 5-30% of emailed commenters denied submitting; most comments at 8 of 10 agencies lacked emails; submitter fields are agency-configurable), so any 'verified signers' claim would re-create the astroturf artifact.
key_risks.mitigation: Hard product rule: never claim verified signers. Geocoding stays aggregate, self-attested, and explicitly labeled as unverified annotation. Bake this into UI copy review so a future PM can't quietly upgrade it into a guarantee.

winners.verdict: It made the cut only as the narrow, anchor-gated, embedded-module form the DEFER verdict explicitly held open — credible because its value rests on the two things the astroturf attack cannot touch (individually-authored comments and a public reproducible method artifact), and honest because it concedes it is a contingent module riding DocketClock, not yet a standalone fundable bet.

votes.rationale: The idea is intellectually honest and well-scoped after the debate — it fills Gaps 1, 4, and 5 cleanly, the "public reproducible method artifact" framing is a real differentiator from astroturf floods, and individually-attested comments sidestep the batch-packet credibility problem. Mission fit is strong: it is precisely the monitor-to-act-to-impact loop for non-experts. But the scoring runs into hard walls on feasibility and demand. Feasibility is 2: the product has at least three external dependencies that are not yet resolved — DocketClock must exist and be reliable (the deadline-misread hazard the idea itself identifies as existential), the Pol.is clustering produces meaningful cross-factional signal only above a participant-diversity threshold that a single 10–60 day comment window recruited through one nonprofit anchor will frequently not hit, and the "graceful degradation" fallback (a structured comment-quality workshop) is a much weaker product that funders won't pay for. The stack is theoretically buildable from open APIs, but the operational chain — anchor recruitment, room moderation, per-campaign onboarding, manual filing receipts, public artifact archiving — is labor-intensive in a way that doesn't scale with a small team. Demand is 2: the target user (volunteer neighborhood organizer who can rally 50–300 neighbors on a specific rule) is real but thin — this is a narrow-niche coalition builder, not a consumer pull product, and the prior DEFER trigger (a funded association/nonprofit anchor that wants evidence-backed synthesis and accepts dissent/provenance constraints) is still unmet with no evidence such an anchor exists. The economics remain structurally weak: "fewer, better" comments reduce visible volume that campaign funders value, the buyer is budget-light and deadline-lumpy, and foundation grants for civic tech tools at this level of specificity are highly competitive. Defensibility is 3: the auditable-transparency + individually-authored-comment combination is genuinely hard for Resistbot or Granicus to copy quickly without sacrificing their existing product logic, but FiscalNote/Plural could absorb it as a feature if it proved out. Gap fit and mission fit are real, but the product is contingent on preconditions (anchor, DocketClock, critical mass) that are not in the builder's control, which is the honest reason the prior verdict was DEFER and this refined version remains a conditional module rather than a fundable standalone bet.

Consensus Comment fills real whitespace — nobody runs auditable cross-factional deliberation attached to live comment periods, and the framing around individually-filed attested comments with a reproducible method artifact is genuinely clever and addresses the astroturf discount. The gap-fit and mission-fit scores reflect that intellectual quality. But the idea accumulates fatal dependencies before it earns a single dollar. First, it explicitly conditions its own credibility on a funded nonprofit anchor that does not exist in the materials — the team cannot ship or fundraise without it, and "contingent module" is not a business. Second, Pol.is clustering requires statistical mass; in a 10–60 day window recruited through one anchor org, the realistic participant count will routinely fall below the cross-cluster threshold, degrading to "structured comment-quality workshop" — which is a worse version of tools already in the market. Third, the economics are structurally perverse: the product's value proposition (fewer, higher-quality individual comments) directly undermines the volume metric that civic campaign funders use to justify spend, so the buyer with budget — the anchor association — has incentive to defect once they realize the tool makes their campaigns look smaller. Fourth, the feasibility hit is harsh because the stack is genuinely complex — Pol.is math integration, real-time cluster visualization, Regulations.gov submission pipe, geocoding verification, provenance archiving, and DocketClock dependency — all before v1, for a user (volunteer neighborhood organizer) who is unpaid and episodically motivated. Defensibility earns a 3 rather than lower because the public reproducible method artifact is a real moat: it is hard to fake and Resistbot/Granicus have no incentive to build it. But Granicus already owns the government-buyer relationship and could ship a "deliberation quality report" add-on that commoditizes the clustering layer without needing the nonprofit anchor model. Net: this is a sophisticated research project with a plausible long-run theory of change, not a fundable consumer product wedge today.

Consensus Comment is the most intellectually serious idea in this portfolio and deserves credit for what it fixes: it eliminates the batch-packet astroturf signature, makes the deliberation artifact auditable and reproducible, and correctly situates itself as a contingent module rather than a standalone bet. Gap fit is real — nobody does comment-period-triggered cross-factional deliberation that then routes to individually-attested official filings, and the whitespace (gaps 1, 4, 5) is genuine. Mission fit is strong because it raises comment quality rather than flooding officials, and the "fewer, better" orientation is exactly the anti-astroturf discipline civic infrastructure needs. That said, three interlocking problems keep the scores from climbing higher. Feasibility is fragile: the product depends on DocketClock (not yet built), a funded nonprofit anchor (not yet identified), Pol.is-style clustering that only produces meaningful cross-cluster signal when a 50-300 person cohort is diverse enough — a condition the graceful-degradation gate itself predicts will often fail — and Regulations.gov v4's rate limits and submission pipe, which has already exhibited 2025 outages. The "headline value" (cross-factional consensus officials genuinely respect) will be the exception, not the rule, making the product feel like a structured comment workshop most of the time, which is a harder sell to budget-light associations. Demand is real but narrow: the volunteer neighborhood organizer who can recruit 50-300 engaged neighbors to a specific open docket is a thin slice; most civic engagement tools targeting this persona have struggled to reach critical mass within a single 10-60 day window, and the self-defeating economics (fewer better comments reduce the visible volume that campaign funders track) remain unresolved. Defensibility is moderate: the auditable public-method artifact is a genuine moat against Resistbot/5 Calls style copycats, but Granicus or a well-funded civic tech org could replicate the clustering + individual-filing architecture within a product cycle if it proved out — the anchor-dependency is both a credibility asset and a ceiling on how broadly the product can spread without being absorbed. Bottom line: this is the right narrowing of a previously killed scope, and it should be built contingent on the anchor existing — but it is not yet fundable as a standalone, and the critical-mass risk means its signature value proposition will be rarer than the pitch implies.

residual_risks: Trigger not yet met: the prior DEFER was conditioned on a funded association/nonprofit anchor that explicitly wants evidence-backed synthesis and accepts dissent/provenance constraints. No evidence in the materials shows that anchor exists, so this survives as a contingent module, not a fundable standalone bet.

Dependency on DocketClock: the only credible way past the deadline-misread hazard (extensions/reopenings/multi-deadline-per-docket/timezone, confirmed by the EPA NPDES reopening, the two-actions notice, the BLM false-positive, and the HUD 2025 submission outage) is to consume a deadline object this product does not own. If DocketClock slips, this loses its safest foundation.

Self-defeating economics persist even in the narrow form: 'fewer, better' comments reduce the visible volume campaign funders value, and the buyers are budget-light and deadline-lumpy. Funding remains weakest exactly where the civic value is strongest.

Critical-mass / 'statistical theater' risk: a single 10-60 day window recruited through one anchor may still yield too few or too homogeneous participants for cross-cluster clustering to be meaningful. The 'insufficient cross-cluster signal' graceful-degradation gate is the right mitigation but means the headline value (cross-factional consensus officials respect) will often NOT be produced, leaving only a structured comment-quality workshop.

Verification can only ever be honest annotation, never a guarantee: GAO-21-103181 shows even the official record can't reliably attribute comments (5-30% of emailed commenters denied submitting; most comments at 8 of 10 agencies lacked emails; submitter fields are agency-configurable). The product must permanently avoid any 'verified signers' claim or it re-becomes the astroturf artifact it claims to defeat.

Residual perception risk: even individually-filed, AI-seeded comments informed by a shared frame can be reframed by an opponent as coordinated AI advocacy. The public reproducible method artifact is the mitigation, but its credibility is asserted, not yet demonstrated with a real clerk/journalist audit.

winners.title: ParcelWatch — address-keyed land-use change alerts with pre-filled comment and a provenance receipt
winners.tagline: "Did anything just change on the land near me, and what's the deadline to weigh in?" — answered by email, with the comment pre-written.
winners.the_problem: A resident who cares about their block cannot answer "is anything being rezoned, varianced, or built near me, and is there a hearing I can speak at before the deadline?" The data exists but is scattered across Legistar planning agendas, Accela permit portals, Socrata tables, and ArcGIS parcel layers, each in raw local vocabulary, none keyed to where you live, none surfacing the comment deadline as an alertable object. Official channels (the city's own statutory mailed notice) arrive late, are written for lawyers, and tell you nothing about how to respond. So the busy non-expert finds out about the four-story building next door after it's approved.
winners.target_user: Primary beneficiary and the persona the product is designed around: the engaged-but-time-poor resident (homeowner or long-term renter) who would weigh in on a nearby zoning/variance/development hearing if someone told them it existed and handed them a way to act. Primary early payer (after free consumer retention is proven): prosumer/org subscribers who already pay humans to watch planning agendas — neighborhood association boards, single-issue land-use advocacy groups (pro- and anti-housing), and small local-news/newsletter operators. Secondary/later payer: cities themselves, sold a SocietyWorks-style B2G notification rail that discharges a notification mandate they already fund.
winners.the_solution: ParcelWatch is a consumer-first change-detection and guided-action app, not a data warehouse. A resident enters their address; we geocode it to a POINT and watch for land-use events within a radius — deliberately mirroring the 300-to-1,500-foot statutory notification radius cities already use, so the matching model is one residents and clerks both already trust. The MONITOR half: an ingestion pipeline pulls planning/zoning matters from Legistar's auth-free OData API, runs RuleBox-style LLM classification over the free-text agenda items and attached PDFs to extract (address, change-type, file number, hearing date, comment endpoint, deadline) with an explicit confidence tier, geocodes the extracted address, and radius-matches it to subscribers. High-confidence hits auto-alert; low-confidence hits are held for human review. We sell RECALL ("no hearing near you passes silently") and are honest that precision is best-effort. The ACT half: each alert ships with a plain-language summary, the file number, the exact submission endpoint, and a pre-filled written/e-comment the resident edits and sends; Pol.is-style consensus surfacing groups what neighbors are saying so submissions read as genuine distinct positions rather than a discounted form-letter flood. The SEE-IMPACT half is honest provenance, not theater: because we ingest the same minutes, we follow each file through "filed → continued to date X → final motion Y" and report what actually happened to the thing you commented on. Parcel polygons are an optional display enrichment rented per-view via Regrid's metered API, never a stored licensed dataset, so geometry stays out of COGS.
how_it_works: Resident enters a street address; we geocode it to a lat/lng point (Census/Nominatim free tier first, paid geocoder as a fallback) and resolve the governing jurisdiction via a MapIt-style geocode->jurisdiction lookup so we know which Legistar instance to watch.

A scheduled ingester polls the city's Legistar OData API for new/updated planning matters and agenda items, downloads attached staff-report PDFs, and stores each as an Open Civic Data Event with an OCD-ID for stable identity and dedup.

A RuleBox-style LLM extraction step pulls structured fields (subject address, change-type, file number, hearing date/time, comment channel, comment deadline) from each free-text matter and PDF, tagging every field with a confidence score; the city's free ArcGIS parcel layer is used to validate/snap the extracted address when available.

We geocode the extracted subject address and radius-match it against every subscriber point; high-confidence matches fire an email/SMS alert immediately, low-confidence matches go to a lightweight human review queue before sending.

The alert renders a plain-language summary, a Regrid-rendered map of the affected parcel (rented per-view), the file number and exact submission endpoint, and a pre-filled comment plus Pol.is-style view of neighbor positions; the resident edits and submits, or RSVPs to speak.
The same ingester keeps watching that file number's subsequent agendas and minutes and pushes provenance updates — continued, approved, denied, conditions attached — back to everyone who acted on it.

builds_on.name: Legistar (Granicus) Web API
builds_on.what: The single opening connector. Auth-free OData covering ~70% of large US cities gives us planning/zoning agendas, matters, file numbers, hearing dates and committees with one integration instead of N scrapers — the entire Tier-1 source.

builds_on.name: mySociety RuleBox
builds_on.what: LLM-generates classification/extraction rules that then run on cheap infra at firehose scale — the mechanism that makes pulling (address, change-type, file#, deadline) from thousands of idiosyncratic free-text agenda lines and PDFs economically feasible, with confidence tiering baked in.

builds_on.name: Open Civic Data Event model + OCD-IDs
builds_on.what: Adopted as the internal schema for every ingested hearing/matter, giving stable cross-source identity, dedup, and the 'event as first-class object' that the comment-deadline alert is built on.

builds_on.name: MapIt (mySociety)
builds_on.what: The 'what governs me' primitive — address/point -> jurisdiction resolution that tells us which Legistar instance and which statutory notification radius apply to a given subscriber.

builds_on.name: City Scrapers (Scrapy) + diff/cancelled pipeline
builds_on.what: The proven pattern for Tier-2/3 cities without Legistar (Socrata/ArcGIS/Accela), and specifically its diff pipeline that marks vanished meetings cancelled — reused only when a paying customer commits to a non-Legistar city.

builds_on.name: Regrid metered parcel API
builds_on.what: Rented per-view for authoritative parcel polygon display, keeping licensed geometry entirely out of stored COGS; free municipal ArcGIS layers are used first where they exist.

builds_on.name: Pol.is / Computational Democracy patterns
builds_on.what: Consensus surfacing across neighbor comments so submissions present as distinct genuine positions rather than an identical-form-letter flood that officials discount.

builds_on.name: SocietyWorks (mySociety) model
builds_on.what: The B2G commercial template: a free consumer product subsidized by selling cities a notification rail that discharges a mandate they already fund — the eventual sustainability path.

winners.gap_filled: Primarily Gap 2 (land use made legible and actionable for residents — the brief's clearest, hardest whitespace), wrapped around Gap 1 (the comment-period deadline as a first-class alertable object) and delivered through Gap 4 (plain-language legibility + guided action) and Gap 5 (monitor->act->see-impact in one product). It is novel because no one couples address-keyed land-use event discovery to a pre-filled action flow and a real provenance loop: coUrbanize is developer-commissioned per-project, Symbium is a parcel-compliance lookup with no event feed, City Tracker AI is NYC analytics with no action, Granicus EngagementHQ is a per-project government silo, and Regrid/Zoneomics sell the static zoning map (the commodified layer we deliberately do NOT compete on) rather than the dated change event.
winners.mvp_scope: One city, Legistar-only, plus that city's free ArcGIS parcel layer. Suggested first geography: Chicago — large Legistar footprint, an unusually rich free open-data/ArcGIS ecosystem, an active and organized neighborhood/aldermanic land-use culture, and dense enough that any given address has nearby activity (so the alert fires often enough to retain). The MVP is: address signup -> point geocode + radius match against Legistar planning matters -> RuleBox extraction with confidence tiering and a human-review queue for low-confidence items -> email alert with plain-language summary, file number, submission endpoint, a pre-filled editable comment, and a Regrid-rendered parcel map -> a provenance follow-up when the file's status changes. Explicitly OUT of v1: the open API, the second city, Socrata/ArcGIS/Accela connectors, SMS, and any consumer sub-brands (Lot Line/Block Whip/Permit Pulse). Add a second city only after demonstrating retention (e.g., alert open rates and repeat actions) in the first.
winners.why_now: Three curves crossed. Legistar now exposes auth-free OData for ~70% of large cities, so the single most leverage-rich source is integrable with one connector instead of a scraper farm. Socrata/ArcGIS open-data mandates have matured to the point that raw parcel and permit layers are increasingly public for free in major cities, making per-view geometry and address validation cheap. And RuleBox-style LLM-generated-rules-on-cheap-infra finally makes extracting structured land-use events from thousands of idiosyncratic free-text agenda lines and PDFs economically feasible — the exact bottleneck that killed prior attempts at normalizing this corpus.
winners.business_model: Free consumer app first to prove retention in 2-3 beachhead cities (no monetization until retention is real). Then, in order of validated demand: (a) prosumer/org subscriptions — neighborhood association boards, land-use advocacy groups, and local-news/newsletter operators who today pay a person to scan planning agendas; we displace that labor with a multi-address/whole-jurisdiction watch + export. (b) A SocietyWorks-style B2G notification-rail sale to cities that already fund mailed statutory notice and want a digital channel that demonstrably reaches residents. The open feed/API is a deliberately LATER artifact extracted from a validated app, monetized as metered access to proptech/civic-tech consumers if and only if downstream demand is demonstrated — never the opening bet, because the brief's recurring death is shipping infra against unproven downstream demand.
winners.defensibility: The moat is not the data (Granicus owns Legistar; Regrid owns parcels) — it is the cross-vendor event graph plus the resident trust/action/receipt loop that neither incumbent is structurally inclined to build. Granicus has a direct channel conflict: it sells EngagementHQ to the city as the official engagement tool, so it will not ship a consumer product that organizes residents to oppose the city's own filings, and it would be reselling its government customers' own agendas back against them. Regrid is a static-layer enterprise data vendor with no consumer distribution, no action flow, and no incentive to chase dated hearing events. The defensible asset compounds in the loop only ParcelWatch runs: subscriber address density per neighborhood, the verified-comment + provenance archive, and the consensus graph of who-said-what — distribution and trust that an enterprise data vendor or a gov-channel incumbent cannot replicate without abandoning their core business.

key_risks.risk: Recall IS the product, and the source is messy free-text/PDF agendas where probabilistic extraction will miss records — a single silently-missed nearby hearing breaks the 'nothing passes silently' promise that the whole value prop rests on.
key_risks.mitigation: Frame the promise honestly as 'best-effort recall + a safety net' rather than a guarantee; set extraction confidence thresholds conservatively (recall-biased) and route the long tail to a cheap human-review queue; cross-check Legistar matters against the city's free permit/ArcGIS layer as a second signal; instrument missed-event detection by reconciling against published final minutes and publish a measured recall number rather than claiming completeness.

key_risks.risk: This is a per-city geocoding + entity-resolution service, not a data pull — the geocode->address->event linkage breaks constantly and maintenance scales linearly with cities, which is the self-hosting-200-scrapers trap relabeled.
key_risks.mitigation: Stay Legistar-only at launch (one connector, no per-city scraper), and gate every Tier-2/3 city behind a committed paying customer so pipeline count never outruns revenue; use the point-radius model (not parcel-polygon entity resolution) so the join degrades gracefully instead of breaking; templatize Socrata/ArcGIS connectors so additions are config, not code; treat decay as an operating cost budgeted against the per-city contract, not an externality.

key_risks.risk: Revenue is unproven: neighborhood associations have near-zero budget, local news is contracting, proptech buys ownership/value data not hearing dates — and free-consumer-first defers all monetization validation in a domain where civic-tech sustainability is the recurring failure mode.
key_risks.mitigation: Treat the free app strictly as a retention experiment with a hard kill/pivot gate before any infra spend; run paid-pilot conversations with org buyers in parallel with the free launch rather than after it; anchor the org pitch on displacing a known existing labor cost (a person scanning agendas) so willingness-to-pay is measured against a real budget line, not invented; keep COGS near zero (rented geometry, cheap LLM rules) so the runway to find revenue is long.

key_risks.risk: Adjacency creep is acute, not hypothetical: Granicus owns Legistar and Regrid owns parcels — either is one product decision from the box, and we are reselling Granicus's own data.
key_risks.mitigation: Lean into the structural reasons incumbents won't build it (Granicus's gov-channel conflict; Regrid's enterprise model), build the defensible loop (address density, verified-comment archive, consensus graph) fast, and stay multi-source so the value is the cross-vendor graph no single incumbent spans; avoid contractual dependence on any one Legistar term by keeping the OCD abstraction layer swappable.

key_risks.risk: Action may still feel like theater: surfacing a hearing doesn't lower the real friction (weekday in-person testimony) and entrenched local dynamics decide outcomes, so the resident who commented and lost feels the exact 'effort wasted' the thesis exists to prevent.
key_risks.mitigation: Lead with the lowest-friction channel that actually counts (written/e-comment with file number) rather than in-person; use honest provenance to reframe success as 'your position is on the record and here's what happened' not 'you won'; surface aggregate consensus so an individual sees they're part of a bloc; be explicit in product copy that the win is being heard on time, never a guaranteed outcome.

key_risks.risk: Demand validated so far is for government-MANDATED notification (a captive statutory channel) — that residents want to be notified does not prove they will act, retain, or pay for a better rail.
key_risks.mitigation: Make the retention gate measure ACTION (comments filed, repeat engagement) not just signups; instrument the act->provenance loop as the core retention driver; treat the B2G mandate demand as a backstop revenue line, not as proof of consumer willingness, and validate consumer behavior independently before scaling.

winners.verdict: It made the cut because it attacks the brief's clearest whitespace (land use, Gaps 1+2+4+5) with a leverage-ordered, COGS-disciplined plan that honestly demotes its own weakest claims — the open API, the parcel warehouse, and any recall guarantee — instead of hiding them, but it survives as a consumer app with a hard retention gate, not as the infrastructure its title once promised.

votes.rationale: Gap fit is real and acknowledged in the brief: land-use change detection keyed to an address is the clearest whitespace, no incumbent crosses the Legistar+Socrata+ArcGIS+Accela stack, and the "diff + deadline" framing directly targets Gap 1 and Gap 2. But feasibility gets a hard 2 because the entire value proposition rests on RECALL — "no hearing near you passes silently" — and the extraction pipeline is explicitly probabilistic LLM/RuleBox over messy free-text PDFs and agendas. A single silently missed variance hearing breaks the trust the product is built on, and there is no reliable way to know what you missed; you cannot audit a false negative in a corpus you don't fully control. The Legistar OData connector covers ~70% of large cities but planning/zoning agenda line items are notoriously unstructured even within Legistar, and the richest detail — addresses, file numbers, hearing types — is buried in attached PDFs that Legistar does not normalize. Demand scores a 3 because statutory notification already exists (cities mail 1,500-ft radius notices) so the incremental willingness-to-pay for a better digital rail is unproven; residents who care already receive paper notices, and those who don't care won't subscribe. Defensibility is a 3: the cross-vendor event graph is a real moat in principle, but building it city-by-city is slow, and Granicus (which already owns Legistar) is the most dangerous adjacent incumbent — it already has the Legistar data and a government-sales relationship, and could ship a resident-facing notification product without a new data contract. Mission fit is only a 3 despite the consumer framing because the "honest provenance, not impact theater" positioning concedes upfront that the busy non-expert resident may still feel their comment changed nothing; local land-use outcomes are decided by entrenched political and developer dynamics that a comment-filing tool does not materially shift, and the product's own residual risks acknowledge this. The free-consumer-first plan defers all monetization proof while civic-tech sustainability is the brief's documented failure mode.

ParcelWatch has the sharpest gap-fit in the entire thesis — land use is genuinely uncovered and the diff+deadline framing is a real insight — but the refinements paper over the hardest structural problem rather than solve it: recall is both the entire value proposition and the thing the data source makes nearly impossible to guarantee at launch. LLM extraction from free-text Legistar agendas and PDFs will miss hearings, and a single missed variance notice near a user's home destroys the trust the product is built on. The "high-confidence auto-alert, low-confidence held for review" tier sounds responsible but it means the product ships with known gaps, which is fine for a B2B dashboard and fatal for a consumer trust product. The consumer-first pivot is the right instinct but it collapses the B2B2C monetization thesis: a free consumer app in 2-3 cities proves nothing to a neighborhood association or local-news outlet about national coverage reliability. The SocietyWorks-style B2G notification-rail exit is the most credible revenue path — cities already fund statutory notification mandates and would pay to outsource a better rail — but that's an enterprise sales motion that contradicts the consumer-first launch and won't close fast enough to survive the sustainability trap the brief explicitly identifies as the recurring civic-tech failure mode. Defensibility is real but narrower than claimed: Legistar OData is already public and Granicus owns the vendor relationship with every city on it; the moment this gets traction Granicus can extend their Notify product into exactly this slot. The cross-vendor event graph is a genuine moat but it takes years of per-city Tier 2/3 work to build, work the idea explicitly tries to defer. Score reflects a genuinely important gap with a compelling framing, penalized heavily for an unresolved recall/reliability tension that is the product's core promise, a deferred monetization model in a domain littered with deferred monetization, and a defensibility story that depends on speed the free-consumer-first plan does not provide.

ParcelWatch v2 makes the right intellectual moves — it targets the clearest whitespace (Gap 2, land-use legibility and actionability), correctly identifies Legistar OData as the only feasible beachhead connector, and abandons the premature "open API" framing in favor of demand-gated source sequencing. The gap_fit score is the strongest element: no incumbent crosses the Legistar+Socrata+ArcGIS+Accela boundary while also surfacing a change-diff with a resident-facing action layer, and the reframe from static parcel warehouse to dated event + deadline is genuinely novel. But three structural tensions drag the other scores down. First, feasibility suffers because "recall is the whole product" and the achievable recall against free-text PDF agendas is limited — probabilistic extraction will miss hearings, and a single missed hearing breaks the trust proposition the entire value prop rests on; this is not a solvable engineering problem, it is an irreducible data-quality constraint inherent to local-gov messy sources. Second, demand is real but conflated: statutory-notification recipients (captive) are not the same population as voluntary civic engagers willing to pay for a better rail, and the brief's recurring failure mode (civic-tech sustainability) is not answered by "free consumer app first, monetize later" — that deferred revenue plan has ended dormant projects before. Third, defensibility is overstated: the cross-vendor event graph is a genuine moat only if the team can sustain the multi-source ingestion and keep recall high; Granicus, which owns Legistar, could surface a resident-facing change-alert with one product decision, and Regrid could add hearing-date enrichment to its existing parcel API — both moves are architecturally easier for incumbents than the idea acknowledges. Mission fit is moderate: the monitor-to-act loop is partially present, the Pol.is consensus layer reduces form-letter flood risk, and provenance receipts are honest — but the "impact theater" admission also signals that the busy non-expert may still feel their effort was wasted, which is the precise failure mode the thesis exists to prevent. Overall this is a well-reasoned, technically grounded idea with genuine whitespace, but it carries unsolved recall risk, deferred revenue validation, and a defensibility story that rests on sustained operational complexity rather than structural barriers.

residual_risks: Recall is the whole product and is the hardest thing to guarantee: a single silently-missed hearing breaks the trust the value prop ('no hearing near you passes silently') is built on, yet the source is messy free-text/PDF agendas where probabilistic extraction will miss records. The promise and the achievable reliability are in tension.

The surviving idea is a consumer app, not the 'infra other apps build on' the title sells. The defensible thesis quietly abandons the infrastructure-leverage framing; if scored as infra it fails, if scored as a consumer land-use app it must compete on consumer distribution it has not demonstrated.

Revenue remains unproven: prosumer/org subscriptions (displacing manual agenda-monitoring labor) and a SocietyWorks-style B2G notification-rail sale are plausible but speculative; the free-consumer-first plan defers all monetization validation, and civic-tech sustainability is the brief's recurring failure mode.

Action friction is only partially solved: written/e-comment lowers the bar, but local land-use outcomes are decided by entrenched dynamics, so 'honest provenance, not impact theater' may still leave the busy resident feeling their effort changed nothing — the exact feeling the thesis exists to prevent.

Demand validation is for government-mandated notification (a captive statutory channel), not for voluntary willingness to engage/pay; that residents want to be notified does not prove they will act, retain, or that anyone pays for a better notification rail.

Per-city maintenance is reduced and demand-gated but not eliminated; Tier 2/3 (Socrata/ArcGIS/Accela) still accrue decay, and the richest planning detail beyond Legistar agenda lines still requires the long-tail work the brief warns against.

winners.title: Lot Line
winners.tagline: Type your address once; get plain-language alerts before the bulldozers — the land-use wedge into an always-on neighborhood civic monitor.
winners.the_problem: Homeowners and renters typically learn about a rezoning, variance, or 5-story development next door only when bulldozers arrive or when a jargon-filled paper notice card gets tossed with the junk mail. The statutory mailed notice (often a 300-500 ft radius) is the only systematic signal most residents get, and it has no map, no deadline reminder, and no action path. People who care about their block have no proactive, plain-language way to learn "something consequential is being proposed near me" while there's still time to act. Existing tools are either government-buyer per-project silos (PublicInput, coUrbanize) or expensive lobbyist-facing platforms — none serve the non-expert resident at their own address.
winners.target_user: Primary: homeowners, renters, and small landlords in mid-to-large US cities who care about their immediate block but are not planning nerds — the person who would have shown up to the hearing if they'd known in time and understood the stakes. Secondary (and the real near-term revenue): high-frequency, high-willingness-to-pay users for whom development monitoring is NOT episodic — neighborhood/HOA associations, land trusts, local advocates, and local journalists — plus B2G/B2-civic buyers (cities, libraries, civic orgs) who want a normalized resident-notification feed.
winners.the_solution: Lot Line is positioned not as a standalone "development next door" alert (which dies on episodic demand) but as the LAND-USE PROXIMITY LAYER of a broader, always-on neighborhood civic monitor. A user types their home address once; Lot Line runs a point-in-radius proximity test (using application centroid/point geometry, never requiring boundary polygons) and sends plain-language alerts for rezonings, variances, and major permits filed within a transparent, user-adjustable distance band — explicitly a proximity FILTER, not a legal-radius claim. The same address and the same MAPLE-style follow->event->feed->digest engine also carry comment-period deadlines (federal/state via Regulations.gov + Open States) and local hearings (via Legistar), plus topic alerts — giving a weekly-digest cadence and recurring value between the rare high-stakes development events. Action is differentiated by case type: legislative cases get verified individualized comment plus organize-your-block; quasi-judicial variances get "right person, right hearing, right 3 facts" attendance prompts and testimony aid. Real-affected-neighbor status is verified via address geocode plus SMS/postal tiers (no paid title data) so the input is weighted above the form-letter floods officials discount. Funding is fixed up front: freemium consumer, advocacy/org seats, and a SocietyWorks-style B2G feed/white-label arm that cross-subsidizes the free consumer product.
how_it_works: Onboard: user enters their address; MapIt-style geocoding resolves it to coordinates, jurisdictions, and OCD-IDs, and 'what governs me' is hidden from the user (WriteToThem pattern).

Monitor: per-city ArcGIS REST / Socrata adapters pull zoning-case and major-permit records on a polling cadence; a point-in-radius spatial test against the user's adjustable band flags nearby items. Federal comment periods (Regulations.gov/Federal Register), state bills (Open States), and local hearings (Legistar) feed the same event stream as OCD Events.

Translate: a RuleBox-style LLM enrichment service (run separately from the core app) classifies each case type and generates a plain-language summary, the stakes, the deadline, and the single action that matters.

Alert + digest: the follow->event->feed->digest engine sends an urgent push for a nearby development event and a weekly digest of deadlines, hearings, and topic matches so the subscription earns its keep between rare events.

Verify + act: address-geocode confirms affected-neighbor status; SMS (and optional postal) tiers raise assurance. Case-type-appropriate flow fires: drafted individualized comment + organize-your-block for legislative cases; hearing attendance reminder + 3-fact testimony aid for quasi-judicial variances.
See impact: the app tracks the case to its vote/decision and reports back what happened and how many verified neighbors weighed in — closing the monitor->act->see-impact loop.

builds_on.name: MapIt (mySociety)
builds_on.what: Cloned/imitated as the 'what governs me' primitive — turns a typed address into coordinates, jurisdictions, and OCD-IDs that anchor the proximity test and hide rep-lookup from the user.

builds_on.name: ArcGIS REST feature services
builds_on.what: Per-city zoning-case layers queried directly for point/centroid geometry, enabling proximity watches without HTML scraping in cities that publish open-data portals.

builds_on.name: BLDS open permit standard on Socrata
builds_on.what: Standardized building-permit feed for the major-permit alert stream; used where available, with honest awareness it stalled at ~12 cities and covers permits, not zoning cases.

builds_on.name: Open Civic Data Events schema + OCD-IDs
builds_on.what: Canonical internal model so zoning cases, permits, comment periods, and hearings normalize into one event stream across sources and jurisdictions (Gap 3).

builds_on.name: MAPLE patterns
builds_on.what: Reuse the follow->event->feed->digest notification engine, the separate LLM enrichment service, and a versioned public archive; improve on MAPLE's weak delivery (mailto:) and near-zero verification with SMS/postal assurance tiers.

builds_on.name: mySociety RuleBox + TheyWorkForYou + WriteToThem
builds_on.what: RuleBox-style LLM-generated classification rules run on cheap infra to compress per-city onboarding and plain-language translation at firehose scale; TheyWorkForYou's concept-expanding topic alerts and WriteToThem's hidden-rep send flow as the consumer-facing patterns.

builds_on.name: Legistar (Granicus) Web API
builds_on.what: Auth-free OData feed of local bills, agendas, and hearings for ~70% of large US cities — supplies the local-hearing leg of the retention substrate.

builds_on.name: City Scrapers (Scrapy)
builds_on.what: Proven Scrapy pattern (and the vanished-meeting-cancelled diff) as the bespoke-scraper fallback for the long tail (PrimeGov, CivicClerk, PDF-only) where no API exists.

builds_on.name: Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register API + Mirrulations
builds_on.what: Source of federal proposed rules and the commentEndDate field to make comment-period deadlines a first-class alertable object (Gap 1); Mirrulations/spicy-regs as the free historical corpus.

winners.gap_filled: Fills Gap 2 (land use made legible and actionable for residents) at parcel granularity and Gap 1 (comment-period deadline as a first-class alertable object), wrapped into the Gap 5 monitor->act->see-impact loop in one consumer product, normalized cross-source for non-experts (Gap 3) and translated to plain language (Gap 4). It is novel as a CONSUMER-side, cross-source, always-on proximity monitor anchored to one's home address — not a duplicate of coUrbanize/PublicInput (government-buyer, per-project silos) or Symbium (permit-feasibility lookup). Honest caveat: the "spatial join against the statutory notice radius" is NOT the moat (radius is per-ordinance, per-case-type, discretionary, and not machine-readable; and address-radius dev alerts already exist in some city trackers and Development.i). The defensible novelty is the cross-source civic-action substrate plus B2G-cross-subsidized funding, with land-use as the highest-emotion acquisition wedge.
winners.mvp_scope: One metro, deep, not a wide thin map. Suggested first geography: a mid-to-large city with (a) a published ArcGIS/Socrata zoning-case feature service, (b) Legistar coverage for hearings, and (c) an engaged neighborhood-association/advocacy scene to seed paying org seats — Raleigh, NC or Denver, CO are strong candidates (both have mature open-data portals and active neighborhood notification programs). MVP = one ArcGIS/Socrata adapter + one hand-curated radius/notice-rules table for that city's case types; point-in-radius proximity alerts with an adjustable band; RuleBox-style plain-language summaries; weekly digest carrying that city's Legistar hearings and federal comment-period deadlines; one case-type-appropriate action flow (drafted verified comment for legislative; hearing reminder + 3-fact aid for variances); and address+SMS verification. Ingest the city's standardized statutory mailed-notice list and accept user-forwarded notice-card photos as the universal fallback. Sell 5-10 advocacy/neighborhood-org seats in that same metro from day one to validate the high-frequency revenue line before scaling cities.
winners.why_now: The proliferation of city ArcGIS Open Data portals plus the BLDS permit standard means parcel-level case and permit geometry is queryable via stable REST/SODA APIs for cities covering a large share of US population — proximity watches without brittle HTML scraping. Cheap RuleBox-style LLM classifiers (and a separate enrichment service pattern) make per-case plain-language translation and per-city adapter generation affordable at firehose scale for the first time, materially lowering the per-city onboarding cost that has historically killed local-civic coverage. Open States v3, Regulations.gov v4, and auth-free Legistar OData provide the non-land-use legs of the retention substrate as free or cheap APIs today.
winners.business_model: Three legs, deliberately not consumer-subscription-as-core (that's the kill-shot). (1) Freemium consumer: free address alerts and weekly digest; paid power tier for multiple addresses, small-landlord portfolios, and advanced action tools. (2) Advocacy/community-org seats: paid seats for neighborhood associations, land trusts, local journalists, and HOAs — the high-frequency, high-WTP users for whom development monitoring is daily, not episodic; this is the most credible near-term recurring revenue. (3) SocietyWorks-style B2G/B2-civic arm: license the normalized, deadline-aware civic-events feed and a white-label resident-notification tool to cities, libraries, and civic orgs, cross-subsidizing the free consumer product. The org-seat and feed lines fund the per-city coverage that the episodic consumer cannot.
winners.defensibility: Thin as a standalone; real only as the substrate. The defensible asset is the unglamorous, hard-won cross-source structured-data + verified-action layer: normalized OCD events across zoning cases, permits, comment periods, and hearings; per-city adapters and radius/notice-rules tables; and an address+SMS/postal verification system that produces weighted, real-affected-neighbor input officials don't discount. None of that is a feature an incumbent toggles on overnight, but none of it is a network-effect moat either — coverage must be bought city-by-city. Against Nextdoor (verified-household distribution) and Granicus/PublicInput (agency-side), the wedge survives only if the verified-action substrate and the B2G feed are actually built and deep in the launch metro before incumbents bolt on "development near you." Honestly: defensibility is execution depth and channel relationships, not IP.

key_risks.risk: Per-city ArcGIS/Socrata adapter cost plus per-jurisdiction radius/notice-rules curation is a recurring maintenance drag with NO network effect to amortize it — the consumer only cares about their one city, so coverage is bought city-by-city. This is the brief's flagged fragmentation failure mode reincarnated at parcel granularity and remains the dominant execution risk.
key_risks.mitigation: Go deep-one-city-first; use RuleBox-style LLM-generated adapters and classifiers to compress onboarding; use the standardized statutory mailed-notice list + user-forwarded notice cards as a universal, schema-free fallback so coverage doesn't strictly depend on a bespoke API adapter per city.

key_risks.risk: Episodic single-household demand: development next door is a near-zero-base-rate event, so the rare alert cannot carry a subscription. The bundle (deadlines, hearings, topic alerts) is asserted to retain but not yet proven.
key_risks.mitigation: Treat consumer alerts as free (acquisition), not the revenue core. Validate retention empirically via weekly-digest open/engagement rates before scaling, and anchor revenue on high-frequency org seats and the B2G feed, not household subscriptions.

key_risks.risk: The 'spatial join against statutory notice radius' is neither novel nor cleanly buildable — radius is per-ordinance, per-case-type, and discretionary, not machine-readable — and address-radius dev alerts already ship (PublicInput, some city trackers, Development.i). The marketed primitive is not a moat.
key_risks.mitigation: Drop the false moat publicly; market proximity as a transparent user-adjustable filter, not a legal-radius claim. Compete on cross-source breadth, plain-language translation, and verified action rather than on the spatial trick.

key_risks.risk: B2G cross-subsidy faces a structural channel conflict: selling a resident-ORGANIZING tool to the agencies whose decisions residents oppose may cap that revenue line.
key_risks.mitigation: Split the B2G offering — sell the neutral normalized civic-events FEED and white-label notification (which agencies want for engagement compliance) separately from the consumer organizing flows; lean on libraries and civic orgs as less-conflicted B2G buyers.

key_risks.risk: Incumbent adjacency: Nextdoor (verified-household distribution + neighborhood map), Granicus, and PublicInput can bolt on 'development near you' faster than a startup onboards cities one adapter at a time.
key_risks.mitigation: Win the launch metro deeply on the structured-data + verified-action substrate before incumbents notice; pursue org-seat relationships that create switching costs incumbents' generic feeds don't.

key_risks.risk: Action efficacy for quasi-judicial variances is inherently limited — owner-of-record mailed notice and hearing turnout dominate, and the app can't verify owner-of-record without paid title data — so the 'see the impact' promise is weakest exactly where stakes are highest.
key_risks.mitigation: Set honest case-type expectations: optimize variance flows for hearing TURNOUT and quality testimony (where residents genuinely move outcomes) rather than written comment; reserve the strongest see-impact claims for legislative rezonings where individualized comment counts.

winners.verdict: It made the cut not as a standalone product (which dies on episodic demand and a false spatial moat) but as the highest-emotion acquisition wedge into a genuinely underserved, fundable cross-source civic monitor — worth building only if the team treats land-use as the hook and the verified-action substrate plus B2G funding as the actual business.

votes.rationale: Gap fit is real and honest: land-use legibility (Gap 2) plus comment-period deadlines (Gap 1) are genuine whitespace, and the refined framing drops the false statutory-radius moat in favor of a transparent proximity filter, which is a meaningful improvement. Mission fit is strong — the address-as-anchor UX for a non-expert is the clearest expression of the monitor->act->see-impact thesis in the whole idea space. But feasibility is the ceiling that caps everything else. The per-city ArcGIS/Socrata adapter problem is not solved by repositioning — it is still a city-by-city maintenance treadmill with no network effect and no shared open protocol to absorb the cost; the BLDS standard covers permits in a handful of cities and zoning cases are almost entirely outside it. The LLM-adapter/RuleBox pattern reduces onboarding cost but does not eliminate the ongoing schema-drift and auth-rotation burden that killed similar projects. Demand scoring is constrained by the episodic-single-household reality that the idea itself names: the bundle is asserted as the retention fix, but the brief's own residual risks acknowledge it is unproven. Defensibility is weak: Nextdoor already has verified household distribution at scale and a development-near-you alert is a product manager's weekend sprint away; Granicus/PublicInput are already agency-side and could surface resident-facing proximity alerts as a free add-on to their government clients tomorrow. The B2G cross-subsidy funding model is structurally clever but the channel conflict (selling resident-organizing tools to the agencies residents are opposing) is a genuine constraint on how far that revenue line can grow, not just a footnote. The idea is the most emotionally resonant hook in the space and the repositioning as a wedge into a broader monitor is the right strategic instinct — but execution requires solving a data-fragmentation problem that has defeated well-funded teams, and the defensibility against incumbent adjacency creep is the thinnest of any idea in this set.

The idea cleanly targets the two sharpest whitespace gaps (land-use legibility for residents and comment-period deadlines as alertable objects) and the emotional hook — finding out before the bulldozers — is real and visceral. But the score collapses on feasibility and defensibility. The per-city ArcGIS/Socrata adapter problem is not softened by the repositioning: every new metro requires bespoke data-source discovery, radius-rule curation, and ongoing maintenance with zero network-effect amortization, because each consumer only cares about their one city. The RuleBox/LLM-classifier pattern helps at classification but does not eliminate the connector layer — and the brief's own failure-mode section flags this as the dominant execution risk. Demand is real but episodic at the household level; the bundling thesis (wrap rare development alerts inside a recurring-value civic monitor) is asserted rather than demonstrated, and the retention substrate (deadlines, hearings, topic alerts) must independently earn its keep — that is a second hard product to build in parallel. Defensibility is thin: Nextdoor already has verified-household distribution and could ship a "something filed near your address" push notification as a feature sprint; Granicus/PublicInput already sits agency-side and can close the consumer gap with a white-label resident app they have been piloting. The proposed moat — the cross-source civic-action substrate and B2G cross-subsidy — requires the startup to simultaneously out-execute on data infrastructure AND sell a resident-organizing tool to the very agencies whose decisions residents oppose, a structural channel conflict the idea itself concedes. Mission fit is high: the monitor-to-act-to-impact loop is exactly the thesis, and verified-neighbor action over form-letter floods is the right instinct. But a high-mission, low-defensibility, high-maintenance idea that requires a dual B2C/B2G go-to-market from day one is a vitamin dressed as a painkiller — the pain is real, but the path to a defensible, sustainably-funded business at consumer scale is not yet credible enough to earn top marks.

Lot Line cleanly fills the two most underserved whitespace gaps — land-use legibility for residents (Gap 2) and comment-period deadlines as alertable objects (Gap 1) — and the repositioning from standalone product to land-use acquisition wedge of a broader civic monitor is the right strategic move, correctly diagnosing the episodic-demand kill-shot. Mission fit is strong: the target user is exactly the non-expert who shows up when they know in time, the verified-neighbor action tier punishes astroturf floods, and the monitor->act->see-impact loop is coherent on paper. However, the execution risk is severe enough to suppress both feasibility and defensibility significantly. Per-city ArcGIS/Socrata adapter maintenance is a recurring, compounding cost with no network effect — every new city is a fresh integration project, and the brief explicitly flags parcel-granularity fragmentation as a dominant failure mode that the repositioning does not resolve. The B2G cross-subsidy is the most creative funding element but faces a genuine structural channel conflict: cities that want higher-quality resident engagement are rarely the same cities with active, contested variances generating the emotional hook that drives consumer acquisition, and selling a resident-organizing tool to the agencies being organized against will cap that revenue line faster than the thesis assumes. Defensibility is thin: Nextdoor already has verified-household distribution at scale and could bolt on proximity alerts trivially; Granicus/PublicInput are already agency-side and could surface resident-facing deadline alerts as a feature extension rather than a product launch. The moat is asserted to be the cross-source normalized substrate and verified-action rails, but those are hard to build and easy to copy once proven. Demand scores a cautious 3 — the emotional pull of "find out before the bulldozers" is real and the bundle rationale is sound, but weekly-digest retention between rare development events depends entirely on whether comment-period alerts and Legistar hearing summaries independently generate habitual engagement, which is unproven and the hardest behavioral assumption in the stack.

residual_risks: Per-city ArcGIS/Socrata adapter cost plus per-jurisdiction radius/notice-rules curation is a real, recurring maintenance drag with NO network effect to amortize it — the consumer only cares about their one city, so coverage must be bought city-by-city. This is the brief's flagged failure mode at parcel granularity and remains the dominant execution risk even after repositioning.

The idea only survives by becoming a wedge of a broader monitor; as a standalone it dies. Scoring must judge the broader product's coherence and funding model, not Lot Line in isolation — its independent defensibility is thin.

Episodic single-household demand still pressures the bundle: the retention substrate (deadlines, hearings, topic alerts) must independently deliver enough recurring value, or the rare development alert can't carry the subscription. The bundle is asserted, not yet proven to retain.

Funding leans on a SocietyWorks-style B2G cross-subsidy, but selling a resident-organizing tool TO the agencies whose decisions residents oppose is a structural channel conflict that may cap that revenue line.

Incumbent adjacency (Nextdoor's verified-household distribution; Granicus/PublicInput already agency-side) can bolt on 'development near you' on top of distribution the startup lacks; the defensible wedge is the unglamorous structured-data + verified-action substrate, which must actually be built and is not yet demonstrated.

Action efficacy for quasi-judicial variances is inherently limited (owner-of-record mailed notice + hearing turnout dominate; no title data without paid feeds), so the 'see the impact' promise is strongest only for legislative cases.

winners.title: RuleRadar for Small Operators
winners.tagline: The proposed rule that's about to change how you run your business — explained, with a deadline and a ready-to-send comment — delivered through the lender, insurer, or association you already trust.
winners.the_problem: Owner-operators and very small businesses are directly regulated by FDA, USDA, DOT/FMCSA, OSHA, EPA, and state boards, but they never see a proposed rule until it's final and compliance is mandatory. Regulations.gov holds the data but offers zero discovery: there is no way to say "I run a 3-truck reefer outfit in Ohio" and get back the open dockets that change your obligations. Trade associations only cover dues-paying members, leaving the long tail of non-members flying blind while only well-staffed incumbents file comments. The result is that the people most affected by a rule are structurally absent from the process that shapes it.
winners.target_user: Primary: owner-operators and very small business owners in heavily-regulated verticals who are NOT members of a well-staffed trade association — the independent restaurant owner, the 3-truck logistics outfit, the home childcare provider, the small specialty-crop farmer. Secondary (and the actual buyer): the aggregators who already hold the operator relationship — community/SBA lenders, specialty insurers and factoring companies, franchisors, trade associations seeking to serve their non-dues-paying long tail, and the accountants/bookkeepers operators already trust on compliance.
winners.the_solution: RuleRadar is a vertical regulatory-discovery-and-legibility service, not a consumer comment-submission app. For a defined class of small operator, it continuously scans the open-docket firehose and surfaces only the proposed federal and state rules that actually change that operator's obligations — explaining what changes, why, the exact triggering clause, and the comment deadline as a first-class alertable object. A two-stage funnel (cheap high-recall shortlist, then expensive long-context applicability reasoning) emits calibrated confidence and the specific clause, so the user never sees a bare "this affects you." When the operator wants to act, RuleRadar drafts a substantive comment but refuses to finish it until the operator supplies their own quantitative facts (dollars, hours, truck/table/child counts, named local impact), then routes it through whatever channel that agency accepts — pre-addressed docket email, print-to-sign-and-mail PDF, or a deep link to the exact web form with the draft on the clipboard. It then closes the loop using GET-only data: fingerprint-matching the posted comment in the docket to confirm it landed, and, when the final rule publishes, LLM-extracting whether the agency's response-to-comments section addressed the operator's specific issue. Distribution is B2B2C: lenders, insurers, franchisors, and associations white-label it to reach operators the cold consumer funnel never could.
how_it_works: Onboard via a partner: a lender, insurer, factoring company, or association passes RuleRadar a structured operator profile (vertical, state, size attributes: trucks/tables/seats/children/acreage/license types) it already holds — no cold-acquisition form for the operator to fill out.

Match: a cheap RuleBox/embedding pass over the Mirrulations / spicy-regs open-docket corpus shortlists candidate dockets for the vertical; a long-context LLM then reasons over only those candidates for applicability (thresholds, exemptions, effective dates, preemption) and emits a calibrated confidence score plus the exact triggering clause.

Alert + explain: the operator gets a plain-language brief — what changes, why it matters to an operation like theirs, the quoted clause, and the comment deadline tracked as a first-class object with reminders before it closes.

Guided comment: if the operator wants to act, the drafting flow collects their own quantitative facts (cost in $/hours, counts, named local impact) and refuses to generate boilerplate; optionally it aggregates distinct operators' facts into one rostered coalition comment rather than N near-duplicates.

Submit via the channel that agency actually accepts: pre-addressed docket email from the NPRM ADDRESSES section, a print-to-sign-and-mail PDF, or a deep link to the exact web form with the draft copied to clipboard.

Close the loop: fingerprint-match the user's comment in the posted docket to confirm receipt; when the final rule publishes, extract from the agency's response-to-comments section whether the operator's specific issue was addressed, and report that back.

builds_on.name: Mirrulations (full Regulations.gov S3 mirror)
builds_on.what: Provides the entire historical + live docket corpus on AWS Open Data so the high-recall matching pass and applicability reasoning run against a local mirror instead of hammering the rate-limited live API (1000/hr/key).

builds_on.name: spicy-regs (Parquet + DuckDB + MCP server)
builds_on.what: Lets the matching pipeline issue SQL/LLM queries over the corpus for cents, and the MCP server lets the applicability-reasoning LLM pull docket text on demand — this is the core 'why now' cost unlock.

builds_on.name: mirrulations-search Postgres schema
builds_on.what: Already models is_open_for_comment, comment_start/end_date, and user 'collections' watchlists plus Federal Register/CFR joins — i.e. the deadline-as-first-class-object and per-operator watchlist primitives are pre-built, not net-new.

builds_on.name: Federal Register API
builds_on.what: The new-docket firehose: detect newly opened NPRMs and comment windows in near-real-time to trigger the matching funnel before the deadline.

builds_on.name: Regulations.gov v4 API (GET-only)
builds_on.what: Used strictly for read operations now — pulling docket/comment records to fingerprint-confirm a posted comment landed and to retrieve the final rule's response-to-comments section. The POST/submission path is deliberately NOT used (it returns 403 for non-federal users as of Aug 2025).

builds_on.name: mySociety RuleBox
builds_on.what: Pattern for an LLM to GENERATE cheap classification rules that run at firehose scale — used for the first-stage high-recall shortlist (which dockets even plausibly touch this vertical), keeping the expensive LLM pass bounded to candidates only.

winners.gap_filled: Fills Gap 1 (comment-period deadlines as a first-class, alertable object) and Gap 4 (plain-language legibility + guided action) for a small-operator audience that no one serves. It is not a duplicate of Quorum/FiscalNote/Plural (B2B lobbyist tooling with no small-operator onboarding, no applicability profiling, no consumer drafting), nor of Regulations.gov (official but zero discovery, profiling, or drafting), nor of Resistbot/5 Calls (Congress-only action, no regulatory discovery). The genuinely novel piece is the compounding vertical applicability knowledge graph — operation-attributes mapped to thresholds/exemptions/effective-dates/preemption — which turns a 70-page NPRM into 'this changes obligations for an operation like yours, here is the clause.' That mapping is what no existing player has and what gets more accurate per vertical over time.
winners.mvp_scope: One vertical, one signed channel partner, federal scope only. Recommended wedge: owner-operator and small-fleet truckers via FMCSA/DOT, distributed through a factoring or commercial-trucking-insurance partner that already holds thousands of operator relationships and is contractually motivated to keep clients compliant. Trucking has the cleanest applicability rules (CDL class, fleet size, hazmat endorsement, hours-of-service, ELD mandates map crisply to thresholds), a famously rule-aware but time-poor operator base, and natural aggregators (factoring, insurance, load boards). MVP = (1) ingest FMCSA/DOT open dockets from Mirrulations; (2) two-stage applicability match against a small operator-attribute taxonomy; (3) deadline-tracked plain-language alerts delivered through the partner's brand; (4) guided fact-first comment drafting with channel-appropriate submission; (5) GET-only confirmation + final-rule response extraction. Defer state boards, coalition aggregation, and a second vertical until the discovery+deadline+see-impact loop is proven with one partner's book of business.
winners.why_now: Three things converged in 2025-2026. First, spicy-regs + Mirrulations make LLM-over-SQL across the historical corpus cost cents instead of hammering the rate-limited live API, and RuleBox proves LLM-authored classification rules run firehose-scale on cheap infra — so per-operator applicability translation is finally economically viable. Second, cheap long-context models make 70-page NPRM-to-plain-language reasoning routine. Third, sustained 2025-2026 rule churn means an unusually high volume of open dockets that genuinely change small-operator obligations. Notably, GSA's August 2025 shutdown of the POST/comment-submission API (now 403 for non-federal users) actually clarifies the wedge: the durable, defensible value was never the POST — it was discovery, legibility, and deadlines, which the shutdown does not touch.
winners.business_model: Recurring B2B SaaS / white-label licensing to the channel partner (lender, insurer, factoring co, franchisor, association), priced per-operator-seat or as a flat platform fee bundled into the partner's existing service. This fixes the three things a cold consumer funnel cannot: CAC (partner already owns the relationship), willingness-to-pay (the partner is compliance-motivated and pays, not the time-poor sole proprietor), and the civic-tech sustainability failure mode (recurring B2B revenue, the SocietyWorks-funds-the-free-product pattern). Optional later tiers: anonymized aggregate intelligence for the partner ('X% of your book is affected by docket Y'), and coalition-comment facilitation as a premium feature.
winners.defensibility: Two compounding moats, both currently roadmap rather than asset — honesty demands saying so. (1) The vertical applicability knowledge graph: per-vertical operation-attribute-to-threshold/exemption/effective-date/preemption mappings that get more accurate and cheaper to extend with each docket and each correction, and which no incumbent has built. (2) Distribution lock-in: once a lender or insurer white-labels RuleRadar into its onboarding and renewal flow, it becomes part of that partner's compliance value prop and is sticky. Against Resistbot-style adjacency creep if/when the POST API returns: they have action infra and Congress reach but not the small-operator applicability taxonomy or the lender/insurer/association channel. The honest weakness: federal-only scope (the clean-data part) is the most exposed, and both moats are unbuilt until the first vertical and first partner are proven.

key_risks.risk: The B2B2C pivot does nearly all the survival work but is asserted, not proven. Without a signed wedge partner, this dies of CAC exactly as the red team argued — it has become a channel-sales motion whose buyers' willingness to white-label and pay is untested.
key_risks.mitigation: Treat the dossier as invalid until one paid pilot LOI is signed before building. Pre-sell to one factoring/insurance partner whose contracts already make them compliance-liable, so RuleRadar reduces their loss/churn rather than being a nice-to-have. No partner, no build.

key_risks.risk: The product partly co-opts the trade associations it claims to serve the non-members of; associations may see white-labeling to reach non-dues-payers as cannibalizing their membership value prop.
key_risks.mitigation: Lead with non-association channels (lenders/insurers/factoring) where there is no cannibalization conflict; offer associations a member-acquisition framing (free tier for non-members as a funnel to paid membership) rather than a substitute.

key_risks.risk: Matching precision is the make-or-break execution risk. One false positive ('this affects you' when it doesn't) destroys trust with a non-expert instantly; false negatives are a liability. The two-stage funnel also raises per-docket cost above the original 'costs cents' claim.
key_risks.mitigation: Show the exact triggering clause + calibrated confidence on every alert and never a bare verdict; tune the first stage for high recall and gate user-facing alerts behind a high-precision threshold (route mid-confidence to a human-in-the-loop review for the first vertical). Cost stays bounded because reasoning runs only on open dockets, not the full corpus.

key_risks.risk: Submission is genuinely degraded post-POST-API. The email/mail fallback is agency-discretionary; some agencies require regulations.gov web submission and may reject other channels, so the 'last mile' reverts to manual paste + CAPTCHA for some dockets.
key_risks.mitigation: Stop claiming to own the last mile. Per-agency, encode the accepted channel and route accordingly; where only the web form works, remove 90% of the friction (find+understand+draft) and be explicit that the final paste/CAPTCHA is manual. Headline value remains discovery+legibility+deadline, which is untouched.

key_risks.risk: The comment-quality invariant (require operator's own quantitative facts before drafting) reduces astroturf discounting but adds friction to the exact time-poor user, capping conversion on the 'act' step.
key_risks.mitigation: Decouple value from action: the monitor+deadline+legibility loop delivers standalone value even if the user never comments, so the partner still pays. For those who do act, pre-fill facts from the partner's structured data (fleet size, revenue) so the operator confirms rather than types.

key_risks.risk: Both moats (applicability graph + distribution lock-in) are unbuilt, so defensibility is a plan, not an asset, and federal-only scope is exposed to adjacency creep.
key_risks.mitigation: Sequence: prove one vertical's applicability graph to a depth competitors can't quickly replicate, then sign exclusivity or deep integration with the first channel partner before expanding. Use state-board coverage (which incumbents avoid because the data is messy) as the harder-to-copy second layer.

winners.verdict: It made the cut because, once the dead POST API is stripped out, the durable value (discovery + legibility + deadline-as-first-class-object, distributed through partners who already own the operator relationship and will pay) is real, unserved, and buildable on existing open assets — but it lives or dies on signing one paid channel partner before writing serious code, and that has to be treated as the gating milestone, not an afterthought.

votes.rationale: Gap fit is genuine: the vertical applicability-profiling layer (owner-operator trucker inputs their profile, gets back 3 open dockets with deadlines and exact triggering clauses) is not built anywhere, and the combination of Mirrulations + spicy-regs + RuleBox pattern gives a real foundation for it. Feasibility earns only a 3 because the two hardest bets are still unresolved: the submission last mile is demonstrably degraded (POST API dead, email/mail fallback is agency-discretionary, web-form agencies require paste+CAPTCHA), and the two-stage matching funnel at production quality — calibrated confidence, correct threshold/exemption extraction across heterogeneous NPRM prose — is an LLM engineering problem that is harder and more expensive than the proposal acknowledges. Demand is real but narrow and latent: the non-member independent operator has the pain but has never had the product, so there is no proven willingness-to-pay signal; the B2B2C channel solves CAC on paper but the entire demand thesis now rests on whether a lender, insurer, or association will actually write a check for white-label regulatory alerts, which is a separate, unvalidated sales motion. Defensibility is partially credible — the applicability knowledge graph compounds and channel lock-in is real if achieved — but both are roadmap assets, not existing ones, and the moat argument against Resistbot/Granicus adjacency creep relies on distribution that isn't secured; a well-resourced incumbent could buy a factoring-company partnership faster than a startup can build one. Mission fit is where this idea loses the most points: the target user has shifted from the non-expert community monitor (the thesis's protagonist) to a small-business compliance buyer — a legitimate problem, but structurally B2B and compliance-oriented rather than the civic "I care about my community and want my voice to count" loop the product thesis defines. The "see impact" loop (fingerprint-match posted comment, LLM-extract agency response) is clever and novel, but it is meaningful only after the operator actually files, which the degraded submission path now makes harder, not easier. The residual risk the proposal identifies but underweights is the association-cannibalizing dynamic: the distribution partners are the same entities whose value prop depends on being the compliance authority for their members, making white-label deals structurally fraught. Net: a real gap, real data leverage, but the B2B pivot trades one set of hard problems (consumer CAC, submission API) for a different set (channel sales, B2B contracting, compliance liability) while drifting away from the non-expert civic mission. Score reflects genuine gap recognition and engineering thoughtfulness, penalized for mission drift and the number of unvalidated load-bearing assumptions.

The gap analysis is sharp: comment-period deadlines as a first-class alertable object plus vertical applicability reasoning genuinely does not exist anywhere, and the Mirrulations/spicy-regs/Federal Register stack provides real leverage. The two-stage RuleBox-then-LLM funnel for applicability matching is technically credible and the per-docket cost bound is defensible. Those are real points. But the idea loses ground badly on three axes. Demand is asserted, not evidenced: the target user (non-member sole operator, too busy to track rules) is also too busy to seek out a tool that requires them to supply quantitative facts before drafting — the friction-reducer adds friction at the critical conversion point, and small operators historically tolerate regulatory blindness rather than pay or click through to fix it, which is why the long tail is unserved in the first place. The B2B2C pivot is doing nearly all the survival work and is entirely unproven: the willingness of lenders, insurers, and associations to white-label a compliance-alerting product to non-dues-paying operator long tails is a bold assumption, especially since associations have an active incentive not to reduce the pain that drives membership conversion. Defensibility is the weakest score: the moat is a roadmap (an applicability knowledge graph that doesn't exist) plus distribution lock-in that isn't secured, and the adjacency-creep risk from Granicus or FiscalNote extending their existing government-buyer relationships into operator-facing alerting is non-trivial — they already have the regulatory data pipelines and agency relationships; adding a vertical-profile layer is a sprint for them. The POST API loss genuinely degrades the see-impact story even with the GET-only fingerprint workaround, which is elegant but unproven at scale and agency-discretionary. Mission fit gets a 3 rather than a 4 because the pivot to B2B2C targeting small businesses diverges from the non-expert community monitor thesis — a trucking company owner dealing with FMCSA compliance is a regulated business operator problem, not quite the same as a resident who wants to stop a rezoning near their home; the product is coherent but it has drifted toward compliance-tech, which is a different market with different buyers and different willingness to pay.

RuleRadar for Small Operators genuinely fills Gaps 1 and 4 with a coherent technical plan: the Mirrulations/spicy-regs stack is real leverage, the two-stage RuleBox-then-LLM funnel is the right cost architecture, and making comment deadlines a first-class alertable object for a specific operator profile is net-new. Gap fit is the strongest axis. Feasibility drops to a 3 because the residual risks the proposal itself names are load-bearing: the POST API is confirmed dead (Aug 2025), email/mail fallback is agency-discretionary and genuinely uneven, and the matching precision problem at the "does this rule change my obligations" granularity is harder than generic docket retrieval — false positives with a non-expert user kill the product faster than with a sophisticated one. Demand is a 3, not higher, because the target user (non-member independent operator, e.g., 3-truck outfit) is real but diffuse; the problem is latent rather than felt — operators don't know they're missing proposed rules they've never seen, which makes pull-based demand weak and the product dependent on channel partners to manufacture urgency.
