Link: join FR document <-> Regulations.gov document via `frDocNum`/`document_number` and docket IDs/RINs; model each docket as docket -> documents -> per-document comment window (never assume one deadline per docket).

Reconcile: run a deterministic rule layer first (FR date vs Regs.gov date diff, date-only vs timezone normalization, `commentEndDate` null while `openForComment=true`, status closed with future date), then an LLM classifier — trained/evaluated on the Mirrulations historical corpus — to detect whether a separate extension/reopening/correction/withdrawal notice actually moves THIS document's deadline (e.g. distinguishing a comment-period extension from a land-withdrawal-term extension).

Resolve and flag: emit one operative deadline + timezone + submission channel, attach every source row and observation timestamp, label the parse path, and set a confidence/conflict flag; route genuinely ambiguous records to a human-review queue rather than guessing.

Deliver: expose REST, webhooks (fire on open/close/extend/reopen/withdraw), ICS calendar feeds, CSV, Slack/email digests, and audit logs; everything normalized to OCD Events with stable OCD-IDs; publish the free 'conflicts this week' feed as marketing proof.
Operate the track record: log every prediction vs the eventual official PDF outcome and publish accuracy on the hard cases — the compounding evidence that becomes the moat.

builds_on.name: Federal Register API + Regulations.gov v4 API
builds_on.what: The two live source-of-truth feeds. FR (free, keyless) supplies `comments_close_on`, DATES text, `docket_ids`, `regulations_dot_gov_info`; Regulations.gov supplies document-level status flags. Their KNOWN divergence (e.g. record 2018-27875: FR `2019-03-14` vs embedded Regs.gov `2019-03-15`) is the literal raw material the product reconciles.

builds_on.name: govinfo official PDF/text
builds_on.what: The legal-reliance backstop. Every resolved record links the govinfo edition so DocketClock can disclaim being the authority while still being useful — the positioning the research mandates.

builds_on.name: Mirrulations S3 mirror + mirrulations-search Postgres schema
builds_on.what: The full free historical corpus on AWS Open Data, with comment-window fields already shipped. Used as the labeled EVAL/training set for the conflict classifier (resolved-deadline-vs-eventual-outcome on past extension/reopening chains). Honest correction vs earlier framing: this does NOT reduce ongoing freshness cost, so it is a classifier asset, not a 'the expensive part is done' claim.

builds_on.name: spicy-regs Parquet/DuckDB + MCP
builds_on.what: Lets the team query the historical corpus via SQL/DuckDB to mine extension/correction/withdrawal edge cases for the classifier and regression-test reconciliation rules cheaply, and offers a path to an MCP-served interface for the AI-agent-builder buyer segment.

builds_on.name: Open Civic Data standard + OCD-IDs
builds_on.what: Internal normalization model. Every resolved deadline is an OCD Event with a stable OCD-ID so reg-intelligence vendors and downstream builders get a clean, joinable substrate rather than DocketClock-proprietary IDs.

builds_on.name: RuleBox pattern (mySociety)
builds_on.what: LLM-generates classification rules that then run on cheap deterministic infra. Applied so the costly LLM only adjudicates ambiguous extension-chain cases; routine notices resolve on cheap deterministic rules, keeping per-document cost near-zero at firehose scale.

winners.gap_filled: Fills Gap 1 sharpened to its only un-served form: deadline as a first-class object that is TRUSTWORTHY. Untrustworthy single-source deadlines already exist (FR native RSS/email alerts, Apify's Federal Register Monitor with `daysUntilCommentClose`, Quorum docket alerts, FRTracker calendars), so the net-new value is reconciliation + confidence + provenance, not discovery. Also fills the real, un-served slice of Gap 3 — cross-SOURCE normalization between FR, Regulations.gov, and govinfo with explicit conflict surfacing. Not a duplicate: Regulations.gov has the field but no cross-source reconciliation or push feed; FRTracker combines the sources but its public methodology describes provenance for obligations/comments, NOT a deadline-conflict resolver; Quorum/FiscalNote bundle deadline alerts inside expensive workflow suites with no clean redistributable deadline substrate; Mirrulations is a raw mirror. Honest scope cut: this version ABANDONS Gaps 2 (land use), 4 (consumer legibility), and 5 (the consumer monitor->act->see-impact loop) entirely.
winners.mvp_scope: Federal-only, US-wide (the one layer that is clean, fresh, and API-fed — deliberately NOT the unmaintainable state/local layer). Smallest credible v1: a daily ingest of FR + Regulations.gov for all currently-commentable documents (~1,000 open at any time per FRTracker's own counter); document-level reconciliation with a deterministic rule layer; the confidence/conflict flag; resolved-deadline records via REST + webhooks + ICS + a public 'conflicts this week' page. Start the eval harness against Mirrulations on day one so the accuracy track record begins compounding immediately. First customer beachhead: law-firm current-awareness/KM librarians and regulatory-affairs teams at FDA/EPA/PHMSA-regulated companies — the segments with documented liability for missed windows and existing manual-verification habits. Do 5-6 validation interviews (the research explicitly flags these as still-to-do) BEFORE building the workflow UI; ship API/webhook/ICS first.
winners.why_now: Live raw data is now free and richly fielded (FR keyless API; Regulations.gov v4 with explicit status flags; Mirrulations on AWS Open Data made SQL-queryable by spicy-regs), so the classifier eval set and the cheap-infra-at-scale pattern both exist off the shelf. Agencies face rising pressure to filter AI-generated comment floods and GAO has flagged Regulations.gov search as unusable for non-experts, raising attention on docket data quality. And the AI-agent-builder buyer is new and real: FiscalNote now markets PolicyNote as an API/MCP for AI agents, validating demand for a defensible deadline substrate to feed agents — a buyer that did not exist two years ago.
winners.business_model: Usage/tier SaaS, priced to the value of avoided misses and redistribution rights, anchored to observed comps. Self-serve API/feed ~$50-$500/mo (beats DIY but above Apify's $0.35/1k floor because of reconciliation + confidence); professional/team confidence layer with alerts, ICS, exports, audit logs ~$5k-$25k/yr (anchored to Plural Professional $5k, FiscalNote SMB avg ~$7.3k); enterprise API/white-label with SLA, redistribution rights, bulk history ~$25k-$125k/yr (anchored to RegAlytics Enterprise $10.25k/mo and White Label $15k/mo, AgencyIQ $25k-$75k/yr). Honest caveat: those upper comps are for full workflow/intelligence products; ACV for the deadline primitive alone is unproven and likely lands lower until bundled with SLA/redistribution. The strongest near-term revenue is redistribution/white-label licensing to reg-intelligence vendors and newsletters who embed the feed.
winners.defensibility: Replacing the dead consumer network-effect moat with four B2B moats, candidly graded. (1) A compounding reconciliation corpus + published accuracy track record on the hard cases — real but slow to build and the only durable one. (2) Document-level + extension-chain data modeling that single-source competitors structurally lack — a head start, not a wall. (3) Redistribution/white-label licensing relationships — sticky once embedded in a vendor's pipeline. (4) Mirrulations/spicy-regs as a proprietary-quality eval set. The honest weakness: FRTracker already combines FR + Regulations.gov + eCFR with strong provenance discipline and ships calendars/alerts/API/RSS — an explicit deadline-reconciliation algorithm is one feature release away for it, Apogee, or any incumbent. The moat is thin and unproven; it lives entirely in execution speed and the track record, not in any structural lock-in.

key_risks.risk: MISSION DRIFT (dominant). This is a B2B picks-and-shovels feed for paid regulatory professionals — the opposite user of the original thesis (busy non-expert residents taking local community action). It fills Gaps 1 and 3 in a narrow federal sense but abandons the consumer monitor->act->see-impact loop entirely.
key_risks.mitigation: Be explicit that this is a deliberate, funded-first wedge, not the whole vision: the federal reconciliation engine and OCD-normalized substrate are the durable core a later consumer/local layer can sit on, but only once a paying customer funds each tranche. Do not market it as the consumer product.

key_risks.risk: Net-new value is narrow and episodic. Single-source deadlines are correct on routine notices; reconciliation only matters on the minority of extended/reopened/corrected/timezone-split dockets — and buyers may treat manual legal verification as non-substitutable for exactly those high-stakes cases, capping willingness to pay.
key_risks.mitigation: Sell as insurance + audit trail, not as a replacement for legal review: 'we flag the dockets where you MUST look, with the evidence pre-assembled.' Price to time-saved on triage across the whole firehose, not to the rare catastrophic miss. Lead with the audit-log/provenance value that legal teams need regardless.

key_risks.risk: One feature release from an incumbent. FRTracker/Apogee/Quorum already combine the sources and ship alerts; adding a reconciliation algorithm is achievable for them. Moat reduces to a compounding accuracy track record and redistribution licensing — real but thin and unproven.
key_risks.mitigation: Move fast on the public 'conflicts this week' track record to own the accuracy narrative before incumbents notice; pursue redistribution/white-label deals that make DocketClock a supplier INTO those products rather than a competitor; keep the OCD-ID substrate open to attract builders who would otherwise be locked into a closed incumbent.

key_risks.risk: Commodity-data price compression. FR API free, Regulations.gov free, Apify $0.35/1k docs — structurally compresses pricing for any standalone deadline data product.
key_risks.mitigation: Never sell raw deadlines; sell reconciliation + confidence + audit trail + SLA + redistribution rights. Bundle ICS/Slack/webhook workflow and coverage guarantees that the free/cheap layers do not offer.

key_risks.risk: Demand is hypothesized, not interviewed. synthesis.md lists validation interviews as still-to-do; the $5k-$125k comps are for full-workflow products, so ACV for the primitive alone is uncertain.
key_risks.mitigation: Gate the build on 5-6 segment interviews (law-firm KM, FDA/EPA regulatory-affairs, association GA director, boutique consultant, vendor/agent builder) confirming a current deadline-verification owner, measurable review-time pain, and willingness to pay for a feed vs a full platform. Ship API/webhook/ICS before any heavy UI.

key_risks.risk: Liability and reliance. A 'deadline-confidence' product invites reliance and enterprise buyers will demand SOC 2, SLAs, indemnity, and security review before meaningful ACV.
key_risks.mitigation: Hard disclaimer + govinfo PDF link on every record; position as research/monitoring aid, not legal authority; defer SOC 2/SLA cost until the first enterprise deal funds it; keep a human-review tier for low-confidence high-stakes records.

key_risks.risk: State/local expansion is the unmaintainable trap the constraints forbid (no normalized cross-state rulemaking API; Legistar has NO comment-deadline field, only free-text agenda-note footnotes).
key_risks.mitigation: Refuse all state/local coverage until a specific paying customer funds a specific jurisdiction tranche, each priced to fund its own upkeep via confidence-flagged LLM parsing with human review on low-confidence items. Stay hardest where most maintainable (federal).

winners.verdict: It made the cut as a fundable standalone business — a genuinely un-served deadline-confidence layer with off-the-shelf data leverage — but it is an honest pivot AWAY from the consumer civic-action thesis, with a thin, execution-dependent moat against incumbents who are one feature release behind.

votes.rationale: DocketClock v2 is a technically credible federal deadline-reconciliation API that legitimately fills Gap 1 (deadlines as a first-class object) and a narrow slice of Gap 3, and the engineering path is plausible given Regulations.gov v4, the Federal Register API, and the Mirrulations corpus as a training/eval set. Those are real points. But the scoring reflects the full set of concessions the defense itself makes. On gap_fit: the reconciliation problem is real but episodic — extensions/reopenings/conflicts affect a minority of dockets, and the gaps the thesis cares most about (Gaps 2, 4, 5: land use, plain-language legibility, the monitor-act-impact loop) are explicitly abandoned. On feasibility: the ingestion and reconciliation pipeline is buildable by a small team, but the ongoing freshness maintenance burden is non-trivial for every docket class added, and the "expansion path" to state/local only on customer request defers the maintainability problem rather than solving it. On demand: pricing comps drawn from full-workflow intelligence products (Quorum, FiscalNote) do not transfer cleanly to a raw deadline-feed primitive; willingness to pay for the reconciliation layer specifically, separate from the workflow, is unvalidated and hypothetically thin because manual legal verification remains non-substitutable for high-stakes dockets — exactly the cases the product targets. On defensibility: FRTracker and Apogee already combine FR + Regulations.gov with provenance discipline; the reconciliation algorithm is one feature sprint for an incumbent with existing data relationships; the "compounding accuracy track record" moat is real in theory but unproven and slow to compound. On mission_fit: the defense's own residual-risk section names this precisely — the surviving product serves paid regulatory professionals, which is the opposite of the busy non-expert resident the brief specifies. Dropping the consumer layer, the verified-resident network, and the B2G cross-subsidy removes every mechanism that would have served the stated user and funded the free tier. As a standalone B2B data business it may survive; as an answer to the product thesis it is a pivot to a different market and a different mission.

DocketClock v2 is a disciplined retreat into a defensible niche, but the defense team's own concessions are damning: the product that survives the debate is a B2B deadline-reconciliation API for law firms and regulatory-affairs teams, which is structurally the opposite of the stated thesis (busy non-expert residents, monitor->act->see-impact). Gap 1 (deadline as a first-class alertable object) is genuinely unserved at the reconciliation layer, earning partial gap_fit credit, but Gaps 2, 4, and 5 are explicitly abandoned, and Gap 3 is filled only in the narrow federal-rule sense. Feasibility is real but not a moat: the FR and Regulations.gov APIs are free, Mirrulations is public, and the reconciliation logic — matching DATES-text to commentEndDate across the extension chain — is a well-scoped NLP + data-engineering problem a small team can ship. The problem is that FRTracker, Apogee, and Bloomberg Law already combine FR + Regulations.gov with provenance; adding an explicit conflict-confidence flag is a sprint, not a company, for any of them. Demand is the weakest axis: the idea asserts law-firm and regtech buyers at $5k–$125k/yr ACV but the research explicitly acknowledges those comps are for full-workflow intelligence products, not raw deadline primitives, and no buyer interviews have been conducted. Regulatory counsel already pays for Westlaw/Bloomberg Law as the non-substitutable legal authority; a caveat-heavy "we show conflicts, verify the official PDF yourself" feed sits in a liability no-man's-land that neither replaces nor complements those tools cleanly. Defensibility rests on a compounding accuracy track record and redistribution licensing, which are real but unproven and thin — one feature release away from any incumbent with existing FR/Regulations.gov integration. Mission fit scores a 1 because the product, as refined, is a B2B picks-and-shovels data feed; the busy resident never appears as a direct beneficiary, the monitor->act->see-impact loop is gone, and sustainability is solved by serving the professional class the brief explicitly wanted to route around.

DocketClock v2 is technically coherent and fills Gap 1 (deadline as a first-class alertable object) more honestly than almost anything in the field — the reconciliation-across-extension-chain insight is real and the Mirrulations/spicy-regs substrate gives a legitimate head start on federal data. But the defense concedes the fatal tension openly: what survived the debate is a B2B regulatory-intelligence feed for law firms, trade associations, and AI-agent builders, which is structurally the opposite of the product thesis (busy non-expert residents, monitor-to-act-to-impact, community). Gap 4 (plain-language legibility) and Gap 5 (the full monitor-act-impact loop) are explicitly dropped; Gap 2 (land use / zoning) is not even attempted; Gap 3 is served only in a narrow federal-docket sense. Mission fit scores 1 because the surviving product does not empower the non-expert consumer at all — it is picks-and-shovels for regulated-industry professionals who are precisely the parties *opposing* community input in many rulemaking proceedings, and there is no verified-resident layer, no action flow, and no impact visibility. Feasibility is a 3 rather than a 4 because the moat claim — compounding reconciliation accuracy — requires sustained human-review bandwidth on conflicted dockets and is one feature sprint away from FRTracker or Apogee once they notice the niche; the "expensive part is already done" framing explicitly no longer holds per the idea's own residual-risk section. Demand lands at 2 because the validation interviews are listed as still-to-do, pricing comps are borrowed from full-workflow intelligence products rather than raw deadline feeds, and the reconciliation premium only matters on the minority of extended/reopened/corrected dockets — buyers in high-stakes cases are likely to treat manual legal verification as non-substitutable anyway, which caps willingness to pay and introduces liability friction. Defensibility is 2: the moat reduces to a track record and redistribution licensing relationships, both unproven and thin against a well-resourced incumbent extending an existing product. As a narrow B2B data business the idea may survive; as an answer to the stated brief it is a pivot, and scoring it generously on mission grounds would be dishonest.

residual_risks: MISSION DRIFT is the dominant residual risk: the surviving product is a B2B picks-and-shovels feed for paid regulatory professionals, which is the OPPOSITE user of the product thesis (busy non-expert residents taking local community action). It fills Gaps 1 and 3 in a narrow federal sense but abandons Gaps 2, 4, 5 and the consumer monitor->act->see-impact loop entirely. As a standalone business it lives; as an answer to the brief it is a pivot, not the product.

The idea as literally submitted does NOT survive: its three load-bearing claims (B2G clerk-funded cross-subsidy, verified-resident two-sided moat, Legistar/state local deadline normalization) are conceded fatal by the defense itself. Survival is entirely contingent on accepting a near-total rewrite.

Net-new value is narrow and episodic. Single-source deadlines are correct on routine notices; reconciliation only matters on the minority of extended/reopened/corrected/timezone-split dockets. Buyers may treat manual legal verification as non-substitutable for exactly the high-stakes cases the product targets, capping willingness to pay and inviting liability/disclaimer friction.

FRTracker already combines FR + Regulations.gov + eCFR with strong provenance discipline and ships calendars/alerts/API/RSS; adding an explicit deadline-reconciliation algorithm is one feature release away for it, Apogee, or any incumbent. The moat reduces to a compounding accuracy track record and redistribution licensing, which is real but thin and unproven.

Raw data is free/near-free (FR API free, Regulations.gov free, Apify $0.35/1k docs), which structurally compresses pricing for any standalone deadline data product unless bundled with workflow, SLA, or redistribution rights the team has not yet built.

Demand is hypothesized, not interviewed. synthesis.md explicitly lists next-step validation interviews as still-to-do; pricing comps ($5k-$125k/yr) are for full-workflow/intelligence products, not raw deadline feeds, so ACV for the primitive alone is uncertain.

The Mirrulations/spicy-regs corpus is demoted to a classifier eval set, not a why-now; it does not reduce the ongoing freshness-maintenance cost, so the 'expensive part is already done' framing no longer holds.
